self._has_logged_first_failure:\n            # First failure - log at WARNING level\n            logger.warning(\n                f\"First Telegram send failure detected at {self.bridge_url}. \"\n                f\"Error: {error_context if error_context else 'unknown error'}. \"\n                f\"Subsequent failures will be logged at DEBUG level only.\"\n            )\n            self._has_logged_first_failure = True  # ✅ Set under lock\n            self._first_failure_timestamp = datetime.now()\n            self._failure_count = 1\n        else:\n            # Subsequent failure - log at DEBUG level\n            logger.debug(\n                f\"Repeated Telegram send failure #{self._failure_count + 1} \"\n                f\"at {self.bridge_url}. \"\n                f\"Error: {error_context if error_context else 'unknown error'}.\"\n            )\n            self._failure_count += 1  # ✅ Increment under lock\n        self._last_failure_timestamp = datetime.now()  # ✅ Update under lock\n```\n\n#### Non-Critical Section (Read Access)\n\n```python\ndef get_bridge_status(self) -> dict:\n    \"\"\"\n    Get the current bridge status.\n    \n    Read operations do NOT require the lock as they:\n    - Read immutable values (bool, int)\n    - Don't require cross-field consistency\n    - Are used for status/reporting, not decision-making\n    \"\"\"\n    return {\n        \"reachable\": self._is_reachable,\n        \"bridge_url\": self.bridge_url,\n        \"failure_count\": self._failure_count,  # ✅ Safe to read (no lock)\n        \"has_logged_first_failure\": self._has_logged_first_failure,  # ✅ Safe\n        \"first_failure_timestamp\": self._first_failure_timestamp.isoformat() \n            if self._first_failure_timestamp else None,\n        \"last_failure_timestamp\": self._last_failure_timestamp.isoformat() \n            if self._last_failure_timestamp else None,\n    }\n```\n\n### Lock Acquisition Flow (Concurrent Failures)\n\n```\nRequest A fails          Request B fails          Request C fails\n     │                        │                        │\n     ▼                        ▼                        ▼\n Acquire lock? YES      Acquire lock? NO       Acquire lock? NO\n (wait for lock)        (queued)               (queued)\n     │                        │                        │\n     ▼                        │                        │\n Check flag=False          │                        │\n Log WARNING               │                        │\n Set flag=True             │                        │\n Update timestamps         │                        │\n Release lock              │                        │\n     │                        ▼                        │\n     │                   Acquire lock? YES            │\n     │                   (wait for lock)              │\n     │                        │                        │\n     │                        ▼                        │\n     │                   Check flag=True              │\n     │                   Log DEBUG                   │\n     │                   Increment count              │\n     │                   Update timestamp             │\n     │                   Release lock                 │\n     │                        │                        ▼\n     └────────────────────────┴─────────────────── Acquire lock? YES\n                                                          (wait for lock)\n                                                               │\n                                                               ▼\n                                                          Check flag=True\n                                                          Log DEBUG\n                                                          Increment count\n                                                          Update timestamp\n                                                          Release lock\n\nResult: 1 WARNING, 2 DEBUG logs ✅ Correct\n```\n\n## Performance Implications\n\n### Lock Contention Analysis\n\n#### Scenario 1: Bridge Healthy (No Failures)\n**Lock usage**: None (no failures, no lock acquisition)\n**Performance impact**: Zero ✅\n\n#### Scenario 2: First Failure at Startup\n**Lock usage**: Single acquisition for ~1-2ms (logging + state updates)\n**Performance impact**: Negligible ✅\n- Only one WARNING log ever\n- Lock is held for microseconds\n- No contention (first failure is rare)\n\n#### Scenario 3: Multiple Concurrent Failures (Bridge Down)\n**Lock usage**: Each failing request acquires lock sequentially\n**Performance impact**: Minimal ✅\n- Lock acquisition is fast (~0.1ms when free)\n- Contention only occurs when bridge is down (already degraded state)\n- Each request waits 1-2ms max for lock\n- Total latency increase: 2-5ms per request (acceptable)\n\n#### Scenario 4: High Traffic with Bridge Down\n**Lock usage**: Many requests queuing for lock\n**Performance impact**: Bounded ✅\n- Worst case: N requests queue, each takes 2ms under lock\n- Total queue wait time: N × 2ms\n- Example: 100 concurrent failures = 200ms total wait = 2ms average per request\n- **Why this is acceptable**:\n  - Bridge is already down (latency doesn't matter for failed sends)\n  - Sends are already failing (no \"happy path\" to optimize)\n  - Logging overhead dominates (2ms lock is <10% of total)\n\n### Performance Measurements (Estimated)\n\n| Operation | Time (without lock) | Time (with lock) | Overhead |\n|-----------|-------------------|-----------------|----------|\n| Successful send | ~50ms | ~50ms | 0% (no lock used) |\n| First failure | ~10ms (logging) | ~12ms | +20% (2ms lock) |\n| Subsequent failure | ~5ms (DEBUG log) | ~7ms | +40% (2ms lock) |\n| Status check | <1ms | <1ms | 0% (no lock) |\n\n**Conclusion**: Lock overhead is negligible (<5ms) and only affects the error path (already slow).\n\n### Alternatives Considered for Performance\n\n#### Option: Lock-Free with Compare-And-Swap\n**Rejected**: No async-safe CAS operations in Python\n\n#### Option: Per-Request State\n**Rejected**: Would violate \"one WARNING per startup\" requirement\n\n#### Option: Queue-Based Delegation\n**Rejected**: Overkill for simple boolean flag\n\n## Implementation Checklist\n\nFor the implementation bead (adc-44u or similar):\n\n- [ ] Add `import asyncio` to `src/telegram/fallback.py`\n- [ ] Add `self._first_failure_lock = asyncio.Lock()` to `__init__()`\n- [ ] Make `_handle_send_failure()` async (add `async`)\n- [ ] Wrap all state mutations in `async with self._first_failure_lock:`\n- [ ] Update all call sites to `await self._handle_send_failure(...)`\n- [ ] Add tests for concurrent failures using `asyncio.gather()`\n- [ ] Add test for race condition prevention\n- [ ] Verify only one WARNING is logged for N concurrent failures\n\n## Testing Strategy\n\n### Test 1: Concurrent First Failures\n```python\nasync def test_concurrent_first_failures():\n    \"\"\"Verify only one WARNING is logged when multiple requests fail concurrently.\"\"\"\n    fallback = TelegramFallback()\n    \n    # Trigger 10 concurrent failures\n    await asyncio.gather(*[\n        fallback._handle_send_failure(f\"error{i}\")\n        for i in range(10)\n    ])\n    \n    # Verify state\n    assert fallback._has_logged_first_failure == True\n    assert fallback._failure_count == 10\n    \n    # Verify logs (count WARNING vs DEBUG)\n    # Should see: 1 WARNING, 9 DEBUG\n```\n\n### Test 2: Sequential Failures After First\n```python\nasync def test_sequential_failures():\n    \"\"\"Verify subsequent failures log at DEBUG level.\"\"\"\n    fallback = TelegramFallback()\n    \n    # First failure\n    await fallback._handle_send_failure(\"error1\")\n    assert fallback._has_logged_first_failure == True\n    assert fallback._failure_count == 1\n    \n    # Subsequent failures\n    await fallback._handle_send_failure(\"error2\")\n    await fallback._handle_send_failure(\"error3\")\n    \n    assert fallback._failure_count == 3\n    # Should see: 1 WARNING, 2 DEBUG\n```\n\n### Test 3: No Lock Contention on Success\n```python\nasync def test_no_lock_on_success():\n    \"\"\"Verify successful sends don't acquire the lock.\"\"\"\n    fallback = TelegramFallback()\n    \n    # Mock successful send\n    with patch.object(fallback, '_send_http_request', return_value=True):\n        await fallback.send_message(\"chat_id\", \"message\")\n    \n    # State unchanged, lock never acquired\n    assert fallback._failure_count == 0\n```\n\n## Summary\n\n### Race Conditions Prevented\n1. ✅ **Duplicate WARNING logs**: Lock serializes check-and-set of `_has_logged_first_failure`\n2. ✅ **Lost updates on counter**: Lock protects `failure_count` increment\n3. ✅ **Timestamp overwrites**: Lock protects timestamp assignments\n\n### Locking Strategy\n- **Mechanism**: `asyncio.Lock` (async-native, non-blocking)\n- **Scope**: Instance-level lock in `TelegramFallback` singleton\n- **Critical section**: All state mutations in `_handle_send_failure()`\n- **Non-critical**: Read-only access (`get_bridge_status()`) requires no lock\n\n### Performance Impact\n- **Happy path** (successful sends): Zero overhead (no lock acquisition)\n- **Error path** (failures): +2-5ms per request (negligible, only affects degraded state)\n- **Worst case** (100 concurrent failures): ~200ms total queue wait = 2ms per request average\n\n### Why This Approach Works\n1. ✅ **Matches FastAPI architecture**: Async-native, non-blocking\n2. ✅ **Simple**: Single lock, clear critical section\n3. ✅ **Performant**: Minimal overhead, only on error path\n4. ✅ **Correct**: Prevents all identified race conditions\n5. ✅ **Maintainable**: Clear pattern, easy to test\n\n### Migration Path\nFrom current (sync, no lock) → new (async, with lock):\n1. Add lock to `__init__()`\n2. Make `_handle_send_failure()` async\n3. Wrap state mutations in `async with self._first_failure_lock:`\n4. Update all call sites to `await self._handle_send_failure(...)`\n","numLines":499,"startLine":1,"totalLines":499}}}

Optional\nfrom datetime import datetime\n\nimport httpx\n\nlogger = logging.getLogger(__name__)\n\n\nclass TelegramFallback:\n    \"\"\"\n    Telegram surface integration using telegram-claude-bridge.\n\n    telegram-claude-bridge runs on the Tailscale mesh at:\n    http://telegram-claude-bridge:8000\n\n    This module provides:\n    - Message sending to active sessions\n    - Result delivery when no other surface is available\n    - Exception push for critical/high urgency items\n    \"\"\"\n\n    # telegram-claude-bridge endpoint (Tailscale mesh)\n    # Configurable via ADC_TELEGRAM_BRIDGE_URL env var\n    DEFAULT_BRIDGE_URL = \"http://telegram-claude-bridge:8000\"\n    # Rate limit for repeated failure logs (5 minutes)\n    FAILURE_LOG_COOLDOWN_SECONDS = 300\n\n    def __init__(self, bridge_url: str | None = None):\n        import os\n        self.bridge_url = bridge_url or os.getenv(\n            \"ADC_TELEGRAM_BRIDGE_URL\", self.DEFAULT_BRIDGE_URL\n        )\n        # Track bridge reachability state\n        self._is_reachable = None  # None = unknown, True = reachable, False = unreachable\n        self._last_failure_logged = None  # Track when we last logged a failure\n        self._failure_count = 0  # Track total failures\n        self._has_logged_first_failure = False  # Track if we've logged the first failure after startup\n\n    async def send_message(\n        self,\n        chat_id: int | str,\n        message: str,\n        parse_mode: str = \"HTML\",\n    ) -> bool:\n        \"\"\"\n        Send a message to a Telegram chat.\n\n        Args:\n            chat_id: Telegram chat ID (int or str)\n            message: Message text to send\n            parse_mode: Parse mode for formatting (Markdown, HTML, etc.)\n\n        Returns:\n            True if successful, False otherwise.\n        \"\"\"\n        try:\n            async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:\n                # telegram-claude-bridge proxy API uses /send endpoint\n                # Contract: {chat_id, text, parse_mode?, thread_id?, reply_to_message_id?}\n                response = await client.post(\n                    f\"{self.bridge_url}/send\",\n                    json={\n                        \"chat_id\": int(chat_id) if isinstance(chat_id, str) else chat_id,\n                        \"text\": message,\n                        \"parse_mode\": parse_mode,\n                    },\n                    timeout=10.0,\n                )\n\n                if response.status_code == 200:\n                    logger.info(f\"Sent Telegram message to chat {chat_id}\")\n                    self._is_reachable = True  # Update reachability state\n                    return True\n                else:\n                    error_msg = f\"status {response.status_code} - {response.text}\"\n                    self._handle_send_failure(error_msg)\n                    return False\n\n        except httpx.RequestError as e:\n            error_msg = f\"request error: {e}\"\n            self._handle_send_failure(error_msg)\n            return False\n        except Exception as e:\n            error_msg = f\"unexpected error: {e}\"\n            self._handle_send_failure(error_msg)\n            return False\n\n    async def send_result(self, chat_id: int | str, result: dict) -> bool:\n        \"\"\"\n        Send a structured result to Telegram.\n\n        Args:\n            chat_id: Telegram chat ID\n            result: Result dict with keys: summary, urgency, data\n\n        Formats the result as a readable message and sends it.\n        \"\"\"\n        message = self._format_result_message(result)\n        return await self.send_message(chat_id, message)\n\n    async def send_exception(\n        self,\n        session_id: str,\n        exception: dict,\n    ) -> bool:\n        \"\"\"\n        Send an exception to Telegram for human attention.\n\n        NOTE: This method requires a session→telegram_chat_id mapping which is not\n        currently implemented. telegram-claude-bridge uses pull-based architecture\n        (manages sessions internally per forum topic), not push-based message delivery.\n        \"\"\"\n        logger.warning(\n            f\"send_exception() called for session {session_id} - \"\n            f\"session→telegram_chat mapping not implemented. \"\n            f\"telegram-claude-bridge uses pull-based architecture.\"\n        )\n        return False\n\n    async def send_workload_summary(\n        self,\n        session_id: str,\n        summary: dict,\n    ) -> bool:\n        \"\"\"\n        Send a workload summary to Telegram.\n\n        NOTE: This method requires a session→telegram_chat_id mapping which is not\n        currently implemented. telegram-claude-bridge uses pull-based architecture\n        (manages sessions internally per forum topic), not push-based message delivery.\n        \"\"\"\n        logger.warning(\n            f\"send_workload_summary() called for session {session_id} - \"\n            f\"session→telegram_chat mapping not implemented. \"\n            f\"telegram-claude-bridge uses pull-based architecture.\"\n        )\n        return False\n\n    async def register_surface(self, session_id: str, telegram_chat_id: str) -> bool:\n        \"\"\"\n        Register a Telegram surface for a session.\n\n        Called when a Telegram user starts a conversation.\n\n        NOTE: The /register_surface endpoint does NOT exist in telegram-claude-bridge.\n        This method is a no-op stub for API compatibility. telegram-claude-bridge\n        uses a pull-based architecture where it manages sessions internally per forum topic,\n        not a push-based model where external systems register delivery surfaces.\n\n        Returns True for compatibility (pretends registration succeeded).\n        \"\"\"\n        logger.warning(\n            f\"register_surface() called for session {session_id} - \"\n            f\"telegram-claude-bridge does not support surface registration. \"\n            f\"This is a no-op stub.\"\n        )\n        return True\n\n    async def check_bridge_available(self) -> bool:\n        \"\"\"Check if telegram-claude-bridge is available.\"\"\"\n        try:\n            async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:\n                response = await client.get(\n                    f\"{self.bridge_url}/health\",\n                    timeout=5.0,\n                )\n                is_available = response.status_code == 200\n                self._is_reachable = is_available\n                return is_available\n        except Exception:\n            self._is_reachable = False\n            return False\n\n    def get_bridge_status(self) -> dict:\n        \"\"\"\n        Get the current bridge status.\n\n        Returns:\n            Dict with keys:\n            - reachable: bool or None (None = unknown yet)\n            - bridge_url: str\n            - failure_count: int\n        \"\"\"\n        return {\n            \"reachable\": self._is_reachable,\n            \"bridge_url\": self.bridge_url,\n            \"failure_count\": self._failure_count,\n        }\n\n    def _handle_send_failure(self, error_context: str = \"\"):\n        \"\"\"Handle a send failure - log warning only on the first failure after startup.\n\n        Logs a WARNING only for the very first failure after startup.\n        All subsequent failures are logged at DEBUG level to avoid spam.\n\n        Args:\n            error_context: Details about the error (status code, error message, etc.)\n        \"\"\"\n        self._is_reachable = False\n        self._failure_count += 1\n        now = datetime.now()\n\n        if not self._has_logged_first_failure:\n            # First failure after startup - log at WARNING level\n            logger.warning(\n                f\"First Telegram send failure detected at {self.bridge_url}. \"\n                f\"Error: {error_context if error_context else 'unknown error'}. \"\n                f\"Subsequent failures will be logged at DEBUG level only.\"\n            )\n            self._has_logged_first_failure = True\n            self._last_failure_logged = now\n        else:\n            # Subsequent failures - log at DEBUG level to avoid spam\n            logger.debug(\n                f\"Repeated Telegram send failure #{self._failure_count} at {self.bridge_url}. \"\n                f\"Error: {error_context if error_context else 'unknown error'}.\"\n            )\n\n    def _format_result_message(self, result: dict) -> str:\n        \"\"\"Format a result as a Telegram message.\"\"\"\n        summary = result.get(\"summary\", \"Result available\")\n        urgency = result.get(\"urgency\", \"normal\")\n\n        emoji_map = {\n            \"critical\": \"🚨\",\n            \"high\": \"⚠️\",\n            \"normal\": \"📌\",\n            \"low\": \"💬\",\n        }\n\n        emoji = emoji_map.get(urgency, \"📌\")\n\n        lines = [\n            f\"{emoji} *{summary}*\",\n            \"\",\n        ]\n\n        # Add detail from data\n        data = result.get(\"data\", {})\n\n        if \"bead_id\" in data:\n            lines.append(f\"📝 Bead: `{data['bead_id']}`\")\n\n        if \"title\" in data:\n            lines.append(f\"📋 {data['title']}\")\n\n        if \"status\" in data:\n            lines.append(f\"✅ Status: {data['status']}\")\n\n        # Add truncated body if available\n        if \"body\" in data and data[\"body\"]:\n            body = data[\"body\"]\n            if len(body) > 300:\n                body = body[:300] + \"...\"\n            lines.append(\"\")\n            lines.append(f\"📄 {body}\")\n\n        return \"\\n\".join(lines)\n\n    def _format_exception_message(self, exception: dict) -> str:\n        \"\"\"Format an exception as a prominent Telegram message.\"\"\"\n        title = exception.get(\"title\", \"Attention Required\")\n        urgency = exception.get(\"urgency\", \"normal\")\n        context = exception.get(\"context\", \"\")\n        options = exception.get(\"options\", [])\n\n        emoji_map = {\n            \"critical\": \"🚨\",\n            \"high\": \"⚠️\",\n            \"normal\": \"🔔\",\n        }\n\n        emoji = emoji_map.get(urgency, \"🔔\")\n\n        lines = [\n            f\"{emoji} *{title}*\",\n            \"\",\n        ]\n\n        if context:\n            lines.append(f\"📝 {context}\")\n            lines.append(\"\")\n\n        if options:\n            lines.append(\"🔹 Options:\")\n            for i, option in enumerate(options, 1):\n                lines.append(f\"  {i}. {option}\")\n\n        lines.append(\"\")\n        lines.append(\"Reply with your choice or type a custom response.\")\n\n        return \"\\n\".join(lines)\n\n    def _format_workload_summary(self, summary: dict) -> str:\n        \"\"\"Format a workload summary as a Telegram message.\"\"\"\n        pending = summary.get(\"pending_intents\", 0)\n        new_results = summary.get(\"new_results\", 0)\n        exceptions = summary.get(\"unresolved_exceptions\", 0)\n\n        lines = [\n            \"📊 *Workload Summary*\",\n            \"\",\n        ]\n\n        if pending > 0:\n            lines.append(f\"⏳ Pending intents: {pending}\")\n\n        if new_results > 0:\n            lines.append(f\"📌 New results: {new_results}\")\n\n        if exceptions > 0:\n            lines.append(f\"🚨 Unresolved exceptions: {exceptions}\")\n\n        if not any([pending, new_results, exceptions]):\n            lines.append(\"✨ All caught up!\")\n\n        return \"\\n\".join(lines)\n\n\n# Global Telegram fallback instance\n_telegram_fallback: Optional[TelegramFallback] = None\n\n\ndef get_telegram_fallback() -> TelegramFallback:\n    \"\"\"Get or create the global Telegram fallback instance.\"\"\"\n    global _telegram_fallback\n    if _telegram_fallback is None:\n        _telegram_fallback = TelegramFallback()\n    return _telegram_fallback\n","numLines":338,"startLine":1,"totalLines":338}}}
{"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"call_ea42016db5844669b9fd6f44","type":"tool_result","content":"1\t# Async Locking Mechanisms for Python / FastAPI — Research\n2\t\n3\t**Bead:** adc-4783 (child of adc-50ld — \"Design thread-safety approach for async FastAPI\")\n4\t**Scope:** Document every viable locking/atomicity mechanism for async Python, with code, pros/cons, and scenario-based recommendations, grounded in this codebase (`src/session/store.py`, `src/sse/broadcaster.py`, the `TelegramFallback` use case).\n5\t**Status:** Research reference — feeds the design decision in adc-50ld.\n6\t\n7\t---\n8\t\n9\t## TL;DR / Correction to the parent doc\n10\t\n11\tThe adc-50ld design doc concludes that **atomic operations are \"NOT POSSIBLE\"** in Python and that `asyncio.Lock` is the only viable mechanism. That conclusion is **too narrow** — it holds for *in-process memory flags*, but not for the codebase's actual shared state, which lives in **SQLite** (`src/session/store.py`, already running `PRAGMA journal_mode=WAL`). SQLite transactions *are* atomic operations, and they are strictly stronger than `asyncio.Lock` for persisted state because:\n12\t\n13\t- They work **across processes** (multi-worker uvicorn/gunicorn) — `asyncio.Lock` does not.\n14\t- They are **non-blocking to the event loop** entirely (the lock lives in the SQLite layer / a worker thread, not on the serving loop).\n15\t- They survive restarts; in-memory locks do not.\n16\t\n17\tThe right mental model is **pick the mechanism that matches where the state lives**, not \"always reach for `asyncio.Lock`.\" See the [decision matrix](#decision-matrix).\n18\t\n19\t---\n20\t\n21\t## The concurrency model — where races actually come from\n22\t\n23\t\"Async FastAPI\" is not one execution model. Three layers can run concurrently, and the right lock depends on which layer your state is shared across:\n24\t\n25\t| Layer | Concurrency source | In-memory `asyncio.Lock` works? |\n26\t|---|---|---|\n27\t| **Event loop** (cooperative) | Coroutines yield at every `await` | ✅ Yes — this is its native use |\n28\t| **Threadpool** (`def` routes, `run_in_threadpool`, sync libs like the sync parts of `httpx`/`sqlite3`) | Preemptive OS threads | ❌ No — need `threading.Lock`, or atomics |\n29\t| **Processes** (`uvicorn --workers N`, gunicorn) | Separate interpreters | ❌ No in-memory lock at all — need DB/Redis/file atomics |\n30\t\n31\tA pure `async def` route on a single-worker uvicorn (the adc deployment: `uvicorn src.main:app`, one process) only has **event-loop concurrency**. There, `asyncio.Lock` is correct and complete. But the moment a code path touches a `def` endpoint, a threadpool sync function, or a second worker, in-process locking alone is insufficient. Design for the layer that actually exists now, but prefer **atomic operations** for anything that is *also* persisted — it is correct under all three layers at once.\n32\t\n33\t> **GIL caveat:** CPython's Global Interpreter Lock makes *single bytecode* operations atomic, but does **not** make read-modify-write sequences atomic (see [GIL atomicity](#mechanism-5-atomic-operations)). The GIL protects against *some* thread races but is not a real lock and must never be relied on for correctness of compound operations.\n34\t\n35\t---\n36\t\n37\t## Mechanism 1: `asyncio.Lock`\n38\t\n39\t### Behavior (verified on CPython 3.12 — see appendix)\n40\t\n41\t- **Non-reentrant.** Acquiring an already-held lock from the same task **deadlocks** (verified: `wait_for(lock.acquire())` times out). There is no `RLock` equivalent in asyncio. If two of your methods both take the lock and one calls the other, you must factor out an unlocked private helper.\n42\t- **Fair-ish, FIFO.** Waiters are queued in acquisition order; a lock is handed to the next waiter on release.\n43\t- **Not loop-bound on modern Python.** The `loop=` parameter was removed in 3.10. A lock created in one `asyncio.run()` can be used in another without error (verified). In practice you should still create locks on the serving loop (via lifespan — see [FastAPI patterns](#fastapi-specific-concurrency-patterns)), because *using* a lock from a task on a different loop than its waiters causes confusion even if it doesn't raise.\n44\t- **Exception-safe with `async with`.** If the body raises, the lock is released (it's a context manager). Manual `await lock.acquire()` requires `try/finally`.\n45\t- **Cancelled waiters don't corrupt state.** A task waiting on `lock.acquire()` can be cancelled cleanly.\n46\t\n47\t### Usage\n48\t\n49\t```python\n50\timport asyncio\n51\t\n52\tclass TelegramFallback:\n53\t    def __init__(self) -> None:\n54\t        self._has_logged_first_failure = False\n55\t        self._failure_count = 0\n56\t        self._first_failure_ts = None\n57\t        self._lock = asyncio.Lock()  # guards the four fields above as one unit\n58\t\n59\t    async def _handle_send_failure(self, error_context: str = \"\") -> None:\n60\t        async with self._lock:  # serializes check-then-act across concurrent awaits\n61\t            if not self._has_logged_first_failure:\n62\t                logger.warning(\"First Telegram send failure: %s\", error_context)\n63\t                self._has_logged_first_failure = True\n64\t                self._first_failure_ts = datetime.now()\n65\t                self._failure_count = 1\n66\t            else:\n67\t                logger.debug(\"Repeated failure #%d\", self._failure_count + 1)\n68\t                self._failure_count += 1\n69\t```\n70\t\n71\t### Pros\n72\t\n73\t- ✅ Native to async; non-blocking — waiters are suspended, the loop keeps serving other requests.\n74\t- ✅ Cheap when uncontended (sub-microsecond acquire in the fast path).\n75\t- ✅ `async with` is exception-safe and readable.\n76\t- ✅ Groups multi-field state into one consistent critical section (e.g. the four first-failure fields stay in sync).\n77\t\n78\t### Cons\n79\t\n80\t- ❌ **Single-process only.** Zero protection across uvicorn workers or after a restart.\n81\t- ❌ **Single-loop only.** A lock created before the loop starts is awkward; mixing it with threadpool code silently fails (the thread can't `await`).\n82\t- ❌ **Non-reentrant** — easy footgun when refactoring (method calls method, both acquire).\n83\t- ❌ Provides **no durability** — if the process crashes between \"set flag\" and \"persist it,\" the in-memory truth is gone.\n84\t- ❌ Holds state in the serving loop: a long critical section (e.g. doing I/O *inside* the lock) serializes every contending request.\n85\t\n86\t### When to use\n87\t\n88\tGuards **ephemeral, in-process, single-loop** state that is read/modified by `async def` coroutines — e.g. the `TelegramFallback` first-failure flag, the `SSEBroadcaster` connection dict, a per-process rate limiter. **Never** use it as the *sole* guard for state that is also in the database.\n89\t\n90\t---\n91\t\n92\t## Mechanism 2: The rest of the `asyncio` primitives family\n93\t\n94\t`asyncio.Lock` is one tool in a family; reach for the others when the shape of the problem differs:\n95\t\n96\t| Primitive | Models | Typical adc use |\n97\t|---|---|---|\n98\t| `asyncio.Semaphore(n)` | \"At most n holders\" | Already used in `src/context/warmer.py:209` (`asyncio.Semaphore(5)`) to cap concurrent context fetches. |\n99\t| `asyncio.Event` | \"Broadcast a one-shot/level signal\" | Signal \"store is initialized\" so request handlers wait for startup to finish. |\n100\t| `asyncio.Condition` | \"Notify N waiters that shared state changed\" | Rare; usually a `Queue` or `Event` is clearer. |\n101\t| `asyncio.Queue` | Producer/consumer serialization | Decouple producers from a single-consumer worker; the SSE broadcaster already uses one `Queue` per connection (`src/sse/broadcaster.py:27`). |\n102\t| `asyncio.Barrier` | \"Wait for N coroutines to rendezvous\" | Almost never needed in servers. |\n103\t\n104\tThese share `asyncio.Lock`'s limitations (single-process, single-loop, non-reentrant). Prefer them by *shape*: don't build a semaphore out of a lock + counter.\n105\t\n106\t---\n107\t\n108\t## Mechanism 3: Module-level / app-level mutex patterns\n109\t\n110\tThe parent doc rejects the module-level lock, but the rejection is overstated — module-level is correct **when the guarded state is itself process-global**. The real rule:\n111\t\n112\t> **A lock must live at least as long as the state it guards, and must be reachable from every code path that touches that state.**\n113\t\n114\t### Pattern A — Instance lock (use when state is per-instance)\n115\t\n116\t```python\n117\tclass Counter:\n118\t    def __init__(self): self._n = 0; self._lock = asyncio.Lock()\n119\t```\n120\tRight for `TelegramFallback` (one singleton instance owns the state). The parent doc's recommendation is correct *here*.\n121\t\n122\t### Pattern B — Module-level lock (use when state is module-global)\n123\t\n124\t```python\n125\t# src/feature/_init.py\n126\timport asyncio\n127\t_state: dict = {}\n128\t_state_lock = asyncio.Lock()   # lives with the module-global _state\n129\t\n130\tasync def cache_once(key, factory):\n131\t    async with _state_lock:\n132\t        if key not in _state:\n133\t            _state[key] = await factory()\n134\t        return _state[key]\n135\t```\n136\tThis is the *right* pattern when the state is genuinely a module global. The parent doc's cons (\"unclear ownership, hard to test\") are really just \"keep the lock next to the state\" — which Pattern B does.\n137\t\n138\t### Pattern C — App-state lock via FastAPI lifespan (preferred for the serving loop)\n139\t\n140\tThe cleanest place for a process-wide lock is on `app.state`, initialized in the **lifespan** handler so it is bound to the serving loop:\n141\t\n142\t```python\n143\tfrom contextlib import asynccontextmanager\n144\tfrom fastapi import FastAPI, Request\n145\timport asyncio\n146\t\n147\t@asynccontextmanager\n148\tasync def lifespan(app: FastAPI):\n149\t    app.state.dispatch_lock = asyncio.Lock()  # created on the serving loop\n150\t    yield\n151\t    # cleanup\n152\t\n153\tapp = FastAPI(lifespan=lifespan)\n154\t\n155\t@app.post(\"/dispatch\")\n156\tasync def dispatch(req: Request):\n157\t    async with req.app.state.dispatch_lock:\n158\t        ...\n159\t```\n160\tThis avoids the two classic module-level bugs: (1) the lock being created at import time before any loop exists, and (2) tests reusing a lock across `asyncio.run()` calls. See [FastAPI patterns](#fastapi-specific-concurrency-patterns).\n161\t\n162\t### The lazy-singleton nuance (relevant to this codebase)\n163\t\n164\t`get_store()` and `get_broadcaster()` use a classic lazy singleton:\n165\t\n166\t```python\n167\t_store: SessionStore | None = None\n168\tdef get_store(db_path=None) -> SessionStore:\n169\t    global _store\n170\t    if _store is None:\n171\t        _store = SessionStore(db_path)   # no await inside\n172\t    return _store\n173\t```\n174\t\n175\tThis is **safe as written** on a single loop *because the constructor does not `await`* — control never yields between the `is None` check and the assignment, so no other coroutine can interleave. **It would become unsafe** the moment init becomes async (e.g. `await store.initialize()` inside the `if`): two coroutines could both see `None`, both start init, and one store would be discarded. The fix is either (a) initialize in lifespan, or (b) guard with a lock whose `acquire()` is the first `await`. Note this is a *single-loop* argument — under multiple workers each process correctly gets its own singleton.\n176\t\n177\t---\n178\t\n179\t## Mechanism 4: `threading.Lock` + `run_in_threadpool` (when you must)\n180\t\n181\t`threading.Lock` is the wrong default in async code (blocking acquire stalls the loop), but it is **required** when shared state is touched from a **threadpool** — i.e. from `def` routes, `fastapi.concurrency.run_in_threadpool`, `anyio.to_thread.run_sync`, or sync libraries that spin their own threads.\n182\t\n183\t### Blocking-acquire footgun\n184\t\n185\t```python\n186\tself._lock = threading.Lock()\n187\tasync def handler(self):\n188\t    with self._lock:        # ❌ BLOCKS THE EVENT LOOP if contended\n189\t        ...\n190\t```\n191\tEven an uncontended `threading.Lock` acquire is cheap, but a *contended* one parks the whole event loop thread — every other request stalls. This is the core reason the parent doc rejects `threading.Lock`, and it's correct for pure-async code.\n192\t\n193\t### Legitimate use: sync critical sections on the threadpool\n194\t\n195\tIf a function genuinely runs on a worker thread (blocking DB driver, sync CPU work), use `threading.Lock` there — it is the only lock those threads share:\n196\t\n197\t```python\n198\timport threading\n199\t_lock = threading.Lock()\n200\t\n201\tdef _sync_compute():           # called via run_in_threadpool\n202\t    with _lock:\n203\t        ...                     # safe across threadpool threads\n204\t```\n205\t\n206\t### The worst footgun: holding a `threading.Lock` across an `await`\n207\t\n208\t```python\n209\tasync def broken(self):\n210\t    with self._tlock:           # threading.Lock, no async release\n211\t        await self._io()        # ❌ blocks the loop *and* can deadlock\n212\t```\n213\tA `threading.Lock` cannot be released by the event loop scheduler. Never hold one across an `await`. If you need a critical section that spans `await`s, that section is on the event loop — use `asyncio.Lock`.\n214\t\n215\t**Verdict:** `threading.Lock` only inside genuinely threaded code paths; never as the guard for an `async def` critical section.\n216\t\n217\t---\n218\t\n219\t## Mechanism 5: Atomic operations\n220\t\n221\t> **This is the section the parent doc (\"Option 4: Atomic Operations — NOT POSSIBLE\") gets wrong.** Python has no CAS for *arbitrary in-memory objects*, but it has robust atomicity at the **GIL level, the SQLite level, and the filesystem level**. For any state that is persisted, these are usually a better choice than a lock.\n222\t\n223\t### 5a. GIL-level atomicity (in-memory, thread layer only)\n224\t\n225\tUnder CPython's GIL, **a single bytecode** executes atomically with respect to other Python threads. Operations that compile to a single bytecode are atomic; **read-modify-write sequences are not**, even when they look like \"one operation\":\n226\t\n227\t| Operation | Bytecodes | Atomic? |\n228\t|---|---|---|\n229\t| `obj.flag = True` | `STORE_ATTR` | ✅ atomic |\n230\t| `obj.n = 5` | `STORE_ATTR` | ✅ atomic |\n231\t| `d[k] = v` | `STORE_SUBSCR` | ✅ atomic |\n232\t| `lst.append(x)` | C-level call, holds GIL | ✅ atomic |\n233\t| `lst[i] += 1` (single elem) | depends | ⚠️ usually, not guaranteed |\n234\t| `obj.n += 1` | LOAD, ADD, STORE (3 bytecodes) | ❌ **NOT atomic** |\n235\t| `if not obj.flag: obj.flag = True` | check + store | ❌ **NOT atomic** (the exact first-failure race) |\n236\t| `obj.n = obj.n + 1` | same as `+=` | ❌ NOT atomic |\n237\t\n238\tSo a plain boolean flag set with `self.flag = True` is safe to **write** from a thread (the store is atomic), but the **check-then-set** pattern (`if not flag: flag = True`) is *not* atomic — exactly Race Condition #1 in the parent doc. The GIL is why `asyncio.Lock` is \"enough\" for single-worker async, but it is not a substitute for a lock or atomic op when a read-modify-write is involved.\n239\t\n240\t> Reproducibility note: I tried to demonstrate a lost update on `n += 1` with 8 threads × 500k iterations and could not reproduce it on CPython 3.12 in a tight loop — the GIL switch interval let each thread run long bursts. The behavior is still **not guaranteed** (the CPython docs explicitly disclaim atomicity of compound assignments), so don't rely on it. Non-reproducibility ≠ correctness.\n241\t\n242\t### 5b. SQLite atomicity (the codebase's actual shared state)\n243\t\n244\t`src/session/store.py` already declares `PRAGMA journal_mode=WAL` and `PRAGMA synchronous=NORMAL`. This gives strong, real atomicity — and it is the correct mechanism for any shared state that lives in `session.db`. Key techniques:\n245\t\n246\t**Transaction modes** (verified working on CPython 3.12's bundled sqlite3):\n247\t\n248\t| Mode | Lock acquired | Blocks | Use when |\n249\t|---|---|---|---|\n250\t| `BEGIN DEFERRED` (default) | none until first write, then RESERVED | readers never block; writers contend lazily | Most writes; cheap but two writers can collide → `SQLITE_BUSY` |\n251\t| `BEGIN IMMEDIATE` | RESERVED immediately | other writers wait; readers proceed | Known write transaction; serializes writers cleanly |\n252\t| `BEGIN EXCLUSIVE` | EXCLUSIVE | readers *and* writers wait | Migration / batch rewrite needing total isolation |\n253\t\n254\t**Atomic check-and-set / compare-and-swap via `UPDATE ... WHERE` + `rowcount`** (verified):\n255\t\n256\t```python\n257\tasync with aiosqlite.connect(db_path) as db:\n258\t    cur = await db.execute(\n259\t        \"UPDATE first_failure SET claimed=1 WHERE id=1 AND claimed=0\"\n260\t    )\n261\t    await db.commit()\n262\t    if cur.rowcount == 1:          # I won the race — exactly one writer sees this\n263\t        log_first_failure()\n264\t    # else: somebody else already claimed it; I log at DEBUG\n265\t```\n266\tThe `WHERE claimed=0` makes the check-and-set a **single atomic statement**. `cur.rowcount` tells you whether you were the winner — this is a real CAS primitive, expressed in SQL. No `asyncio.Lock` needed, and it is correct across workers and restarts.\n267\t\n268\t**Idempotent inserts** already used in this codebase:\n269\t- `INSERT OR IGNORE` — `create_session`, `link_intent_to_topic` (no-op if the PK / unique already exists).\n270\t- `INSERT OR REPLACE` — `set_topic_context` (clobber-and-replace is atomic).\n271\t- `UPDATE ... SET v = v + 1` — atomic increment inside one statement (the DB engine holds the row lock for the duration; no lost update, unlike Python `+=`).\n272\t\n273\t**Concrete codebase race this fixes.** `SessionStore.find_or_create_topic()` (`store.py:444`) has a classic TOCTOU bug — it does a `SELECT` in one connection, then a separate `INSERT` in another, with an `await` in between:\n274\t\n275\t```python\n276\tasync with aiosqlite.connect(self.db_path) as db:\n277\t    ...  # SELECT: no row found\n278\t# ← another concurrent call can land here, also see \"no row\"\n279\ttopic_id = await self.create_topic(...)   # both create → duplicate topic\n280\t```\n281\tThere is no unique constraint on `(label, session_id)`, so two concurrent dispatches for the same new topic can each create a topic row. The atomic fix is a **unique index + `INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING ... RETURNING`** (SQLite ≥ 3.24; bundled sqlite3 on this box is 3.12-era so it supports it):\n282\t\n283\t```sql\n284\tCREATE UNIQUE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS uq_topic_session_label\n285\t  ON topics(label, session_id) WHERE scope='session' AND archived_at IS NULL;\n286\t```\n287\t```python\n288\tasync with aiosqlite.connect(self.db_path) as db:\n289\t    cur = await db.execute(\n290\t        \"\"\"INSERT INTO topics (id, label, scope, session_id, created_at, last_active)\n291\t           VALUES (?,?,?,?,?,?)\n292\t           ON CONFLICT(label, session_id) DO NOTHING\n293\t           RETURNING id\"\"\",\n294\t        (tid, label, \"session\", session_id, now, now),\n295\t    )\n296\t    row = await cur.fetchone()\n297\t    if row:           # we created it\n298\t        return row[0], True\n299\t    # else it already existed — fetch it\n300\t    ...\n301\t```\n302\tOne statement, no lock, correct under all three concurrency layers. This is the single most useful pattern in the codebase.\n303\t\n304\t### 5c. Atomic file writes (`os.replace`)\n305\t\n306\tWhen the \"store\" is a file (config snapshots, the `plan.md` / notes, a JSON cache on disk), the atomic pattern is **write to a temp file in the same directory, then `os.replace`** (verified atomic on POSIX and Windows for same-filesystem renames):\n307\t\n308\t```python\n309\timport os, tempfile\n310\tdef atomic_write(path: str, data: bytes) -> None:\n311\t    fd, tmp = tempfile.mkstemp(dir=os.path.dirname(path), prefix=\".tmp-\")\n312\t    try:\n313\t        with os.fdopen(fd, \"wb\") as f:\n314\t            f.write(data); f.flush(); os.fsync(f.fileno())\n315\t        os.replace(tmp, path)        # atomic rename — readers never see a partial file\n316\t    except BaseException:\n317\t        os.unlink(tmp); raise\n318\t```\n319\t`os.replace` overwrites atomically; a crash leaves either the old file or the new file, never a half-written one. (Same filesystem required — `os.replace` across filesystems falls back to copy+delete and is not atomic.)\n320\t\n321\t---\n322\t\n323\t## FastAPI-specific concurrency patterns\n324\t\n325\t### `async def` vs `def` endpoints — the implicit threadpool\n326\t\n327\tFastAPI runs `async def` endpoints **directly on the event loop**; `def` endpoints are dispatched to a **threadpool** (Starlette's `anyio` worker, default 40 tokens). This is the single most important FastAPI concurrency fact:\n328\t\n329\t- A `def` endpoint touching shared in-memory state races against **threads**, not coroutines. `asyncio.Lock` does **not** protect it — it can't even be acquired from a sync function.\n330\t- If the same shared object is touched by both an `async def` route and a `def` route (or a `BackgroundTask` that is a sync function), you have **mixed concurrency layers** and must use an atomic operation (DB) or a `threading.Lock` for the thread side — `asyncio.Lock` silently covers only half the access.\n331\t\n332\t**Implication for adc:** all the first-failure / persistence paths are `async def` and single-worker, so `asyncio.Lock` is *locally* sufficient — but only as long as those constraints hold. Persisting the state atomically in SQLite (5b) is more robust and removes the constraint entirely.\n333\t\n334\t### `run_in_threadpool` / `anyio.to_thread.run_sync`\n335\t\n336\tFor blocking calls inside `async def` (sync `httpx`, `requests`, blocking file/CPU work):\n337\t\n338\t```python\n339\tfrom fastapi.concurrency import run_in_threadpool\n340\tresult = await run_in_threadpool(blocking_fn, arg1, arg2)\n341\t```\n342\tThe function runs on a worker thread — shared state it touches needs thread-layer synchronization (5a/5b/`threading.Lock`), not `asyncio.Lock`.\n343\t\n344\t### Lifespan handlers — the right place to create locks\n345\t\n346\tCreate process-wide locks and singletons in `lifespan`, not at module import. They are then bound to the serving loop and initialized exactly once (replacing the fragile lazy-singleton pattern):\n347\t\n348\t```python\n349\t@asynccontextmanager\n350\tasync def lifespan(app: FastAPI):\n351\t    app.state.store = await init_store()\n352\t    app.state.telegram = TelegramFallback()      # its asyncio.Lock binds to this loop\n353\t    app.state.dispatch_lock = asyncio.Lock()\n354\t    yield\n355\t    await app.state.store.close()\n356\t```\n357\t\n358\t### Injecting locks via `Depends` / `app.state`\n359\t\n360\tRather than a module global, expose the lock through `request.app.state` or a dependency so it is testable and its lifetime is tied to the app:\n361\t\n362\t```python\n363\tdef get_dispatch_lock(request: Request) -> asyncio.Lock:\n364\t    return request.app.state.dispatch_lock\n365\t\n366\t@app.post(\"/dispatch\")\n367\tasync def dispatch(_lock: asyncio.Lock = Depends(get_dispatch_lock)):\n368\t    async with _lock: ...\n369\t```\n370\t\n371\t### `BackgroundTasks` vs `asyncio.create_task`\n372\t\n373\t- FastAPI `BackgroundTasks` run *after the response is sent*; a sync task runs on the threadpool, an async task on the loop. Shared-state rules above apply.\n374\t- `asyncio.create_task` runs on the loop immediately and concurrently with the handler. Anything both touch needs an `asyncio.Lock` (loop layer) or an atomic op.\n375\t\n376\t### Multi-worker deployment — the hard boundary\n377\t\n378\t`uvicorn --workers N` / `gunicorn -w N` runs N independent processes. **No in-memory lock, `asyncio` or `threading`, crosses this boundary.** If adc is ever scaled to multiple workers (currently it is single-process: `uvicorn src.main:app`), every in-memory lock silently stops coordinating. The only mechanisms that survive are the **atomic operations** in 5b (SQLite) or 5c (files), or an external coordinator (Redis `SETNX`, Postgres advisory locks). Designing persisted state around SQLite atomics from the start means the move to multi-worker is free.\n379\t\n380\t---\n381\t\n382\t## Decision matrix\n383\t\n384\t| State lives… | Concurrency layer(s) touching it | Recommended mechanism |\n385\t|---|---|---|\n386\t| In-memory, async-only (`TelegramFallback` flag) | Event loop | `asyncio.Lock` (instance or `app.state`) — *or* persist to DB if you want durability |\n387\t| In-memory, touched by `def` routes / threadpool | Threads | `threading.Lock` for the threaded section; or move state into SQLite |\n388\t| Module-global ephemeral cache | Event loop | Module-level `asyncio.Lock` next to the data, or lifespan `app.state` lock |\n389\t| In SQLite (`session.db`) | Any / multi-worker | **SQLite atomicity**: `BEGIN IMMEDIATE`, `UPDATE…WHERE`+rowcount, `INSERT OR IGNORE`/`ON CONFLICT`. *No Python lock needed.* |\n390\t| On disk (files) | Any / multi-worker | **`os.replace` temp-then-rename** (5c) |\n391\t| Rate limiting / distributed | Multi-process / multi-host | External: Redis `SET NX EX`, or Postgres advisory lock |\n392\t\n393\t### For adc-50ld's first-failure state specifically\n394\t\n395\t- If the requirement is strictly \"one WARNING per process lifetime, in-memory only\" → `asyncio.Lock` on the singleton (the parent doc's recommendation is correct and minimal).\n396\t- If the requirement is \"one WARNING per *deployment* across restarts/workers, with an accurate failure count\" → store `first_failure_at` / `failure_count` as rows in SQLite and use `UPDATE…WHERE claimed=0` + `rowcount` (5b). Durable, multi-worker-safe, and no loop blocking — strictly better, at the cost of one extra column/table.\n397\t\n398\t### For the SessionStore\n399\t\n400\t- Keep WAL + `synchronous=NORMAL` (correct as-is).\n401\t- Fix the `find_or_create_topic` TOCTOU with a unique index + `ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING … RETURNING` (5b) — this is a real latent bug, not theoretical.\n402\t- The `get_store()` / `get_broadcaster()` lazy singletons are safe *because* their constructors don't `await`; keep them that way or move init to lifespan.\n403\t\n404\t---\n405\t\n406\t## Appendix: verified behavior (CPython 3.12.12, this box)\n407\t\n408\tRan a probe script to confirm the non-obvious claims rather than rely on memory:\n409\t\n410\t| Claim | Result |\n411\t|---|---|\n412\t| `asyncio.Lock` is non-reentrant | ✅ Confirmed — second acquire in same task deadlocks (timed out via `wait_for`) |\n413\t| Reusing a lock created in one `asyncio.run()` inside another | ✅ Works on 3.12 (no `loop` binding error) — though still create on the serving loop |\n414\t| `+=` on a shared int loses updates | ⚠️ Not reproduced in a tight loop on 3.12 (GIL burst scheduling), but **not guaranteed atomic** — documented as such |\n415\t| `BEGIN IMMEDIATE` / `BEGIN EXCLUSIVE` accepted | ✅ Both work |\n416\t| `UPDATE … WHERE cond` as CAS via `rowcount` | ✅ First call `rowcount=1`, second `rowcount=0` |\n417\t| `os.replace` atomic rename | ✅ Final present, temp gone |\n418\t\n419\t---\n420\t\n421\t## References\n422\t\n423\t- CPython docs — `asyncio` synchronization primitives: `Lock`, `Semaphore`, `Event`, `Condition`, `Queue`\n424\t- CPython FAQ on thread state / GIL atomicity (single-bytecode atomicity; compound ops not guaranteed)\n425\t- SQLite docs — transaction modes (`DEFERRED`/`IMMEDIATE`/`EXCLUSIVE`), WAL mode, `ON CONFLICT` upsert (≥3.24), `RETURNING` (≥3.35)\n426\t- aiosqlite — async wrapper over sqlite3; one connection per `async with`, so transactions must stay within a single `connect()` block\n427\t- FastAPI / Starlette — `async def` runs on the loop, `def` runs on the anyio threadpool (default 40 tokens); `run_in_threadpool`; lifespan handlers\n428\t- Related design docs in this repo: `notes/adc-50ld-thread-safety-approach.md` (parent), `notes/adc-4ol5-race-conditions.md`, `notes/adc-2duz-state-storage-design.md`, `notes/adc-65l3-first-failure-state-structure.md`\n429\t"}]},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"da5125d1-116e-4164-b804-5e12910ca7dd","uuid":"19908fa5-f244-4b1d-a079-196af5e00f78","timestamp":"2026-07-19T21:35:28.502Z","tool_use_result":{"type":"text","file":{"filePath":"/home/coding/aide-de-camp/notes/adc-4783.md","content":"# Async Locking Mechanisms for Python / FastAPI — Research\n\n**Bead:** adc-4783 (child of adc-50ld — \"Design thread-safety approach for async FastAPI\")\n**Scope:** Document every viable locking/atomicity mechanism for async Python, with code, pros/cons, and scenario-based recommendations, grounded in this codebase (`src/session/store.py`, `src/sse/broadcaster.py`, the `TelegramFallback` use case).\n**Status:** Research reference — feeds the design decision in adc-50ld.\n\n---\n\n## TL;DR / Correction to the parent doc\n\nThe adc-50ld design doc concludes that **atomic operations are \"NOT POSSIBLE\"** in Python and that `asyncio.Lock` is the only viable mechanism. That conclusion is **too narrow** — it holds for *in-process memory flags*, but not for the codebase's actual shared state, which lives in **SQLite** (`src/session/store.py`, already running `PRAGMA journal_mode=WAL`). SQLite transactions *are* atomic operations, and they are strictly stronger than `asyncio.Lock` for persisted state because:\n\n- They work **across processes** (multi-worker uvicorn/gunicorn) — `asyncio.Lock` does not.\n- They are **non-blocking to the event loop** entirely (the lock lives in the SQLite layer / a worker thread, not on the serving loop).\n- They survive restarts; in-memory locks do not.\n\nThe right mental model is **pick the mechanism that matches where the state lives**, not \"always reach for `asyncio.Lock`.\" See the [decision matrix](#decision-matrix).\n\n---\n\n## The concurrency model — where races actually come from\n\n\"Async FastAPI\" is not one execution model. Three layers can run concurrently, and the right lock depends on which layer your state is shared across:\n\n| Layer | Concurrency source | In-memory `asyncio.Lock` works? |\n|---|---|---|\n| **Event loop** (cooperative) | Coroutines yield at every `await` | ✅ Yes — this is its native use |\n| **Threadpool** (`def` routes, `run_in_threadpool`, sync libs like the sync parts of `httpx`/`sqlite3`) | Preemptive OS threads | ❌ No — need `threading.Lock`, or atomics |\n| **Processes** (`uvicorn --workers N`, gunicorn) | Separate interpreters | ❌ No in-memory lock at all — need DB/Redis/file atomics |\n\nA pure `async def` route on a single-worker uvicorn (the adc deployment: `uvicorn src.main:app`, one process) only has **event-loop concurrency**. There, `asyncio.Lock` is correct and complete. But the moment a code path touches a `def` endpoint, a threadpool sync function, or a second worker, in-process locking alone is insufficient. Design for the layer that actually exists now, but prefer **atomic operations** for anything that is *also* persisted — it is correct under all three layers at once.\n\n> **GIL caveat:** CPython's Global Interpreter Lock makes *single bytecode* operations atomic, but does **not** make read-modify-write sequences atomic (see [GIL atomicity](#mechanism-5-atomic-operations)). The GIL protects against *some* thread races but is not a real lock and must never be relied on for correctness of compound operations.\n\n---\n\n## Mechanism 1: `asyncio.Lock`\n\n### Behavior (verified on CPython 3.12 — see appendix)\n\n- **Non-reentrant.** Acquiring an already-held lock from the same task **deadlocks** (verified: `wait_for(lock.acquire())` times out). There is no `RLock` equivalent in asyncio. If two of your methods both take the lock and one calls the other, you must factor out an unlocked private helper.\n- **Fair-ish, FIFO.** Waiters are queued in acquisition order; a lock is handed to the next waiter on release.\n- **Not loop-bound on modern Python.** The `loop=` parameter was removed in 3.10. A lock created in one `asyncio.run()` can be used in another without error (verified). In practice you should still create locks on the serving loop (via lifespan — see [FastAPI patterns](#fastapi-specific-concurrency-patterns)), because *using* a lock from a task on a different loop than its waiters causes confusion even if it doesn't raise.\n- **Exception-safe with `async with`.** If the body raises, the lock is released (it's a context manager). Manual `await lock.acquire()` requires `try/finally`.\n- **Cancelled waiters don't corrupt state.** A task waiting on `lock.acquire()` can be cancelled cleanly.\n\n### Usage\n\n```python\nimport asyncio\n\nclass TelegramFallback:\n    def __init__(self) -> None:\n        self._has_logged_first_failure = False\n        self._failure_count = 0\n        self._first_failure_ts = None\n        self._lock = asyncio.Lock()  # guards the four fields above as one unit\n\n    async def _handle_send_failure(self, error_context: str = \"\") -> None:\n        async with self._lock:  # serializes check-then-act across concurrent awaits\n            if not self._has_logged_first_failure:\n                logger.warning(\"First Telegram send failure: %s\", error_context)\n                self._has_logged_first_failure = True\n                self._first_failure_ts = datetime.now()\n                self._failure_count = 1\n            else:\n                logger.debug(\"Repeated failure #%d\", self._failure_count + 1)\n                self._failure_count += 1\n```\n\n### Pros\n\n- ✅ Native to async; non-blocking — waiters are suspended, the loop keeps serving other requests.\n- ✅ Cheap when uncontended (sub-microsecond acquire in the fast path).\n- ✅ `async with` is exception-safe and readable.\n- ✅ Groups multi-field state into one consistent critical section (e.g. the four first-failure fields stay in sync).\n\n### Cons\n\n- ❌ **Single-process only.** Zero protection across uvicorn workers or after a restart.\n- ❌ **Single-loop only.** A lock created before the loop starts is awkward; mixing it with threadpool code silently fails (the thread can't `await`).\n- ❌ **Non-reentrant** — easy footgun when refactoring (method calls method, both acquire).\n- ❌ Provides **no durability** — if the process crashes between \"set flag\" and \"persist it,\" the in-memory truth is gone.\n- ❌ Holds state in the serving loop: a long critical section (e.g. doing I/O *inside* the lock) serializes every contending request.\n\n### When to use\n\nGuards **ephemeral, in-process, single-loop** state that is read/modified by `async def` coroutines — e.g. the `TelegramFallback` first-failure flag, the `SSEBroadcaster` connection dict, a per-process rate limiter. **Never** use it as the *sole* guard for state that is also in the database.\n\n---\n\n## Mechanism 2: The rest of the `asyncio` primitives family\n\n`asyncio.Lock` is one tool in a family; reach for the others when the shape of the problem differs:\n\n| Primitive | Models | Typical adc use |\n|---|---|---|\n| `asyncio.Semaphore(n)` | \"At most n holders\" | Already used in `src/context/warmer.py:209` (`asyncio.Semaphore(5)`) to cap concurrent context fetches. |\n| `asyncio.Event` | \"Broadcast a one-shot/level signal\" | Signal \"store is initialized\" so request handlers wait for startup to finish. |\n| `asyncio.Condition` | \"Notify N waiters that shared state changed\" | Rare; usually a `Queue` or `Event` is clearer. |\n| `asyncio.Queue` | Producer/consumer serialization | Decouple producers from a single-consumer worker; the SSE broadcaster already uses one `Queue` per connection (`src/sse/broadcaster.py:27`). |\n| `asyncio.Barrier` | \"Wait for N coroutines to rendezvous\" | Almost never needed in servers. |\n\nThese share `asyncio.Lock`'s limitations (single-process, single-loop, non-reentrant). Prefer them by *shape*: don't build a semaphore out of a lock + counter.\n\n---\n\n## Mechanism 3: Module-level / app-level mutex patterns\n\nThe parent doc rejects the module-level lock, but the rejection is overstated — module-level is correct **when the guarded state is itself process-global**. The real rule:\n\n> **A lock must live at least as long as the state it guards, and must be reachable from every code path that touches that state.**\n\n### Pattern A — Instance lock (use when state is per-instance)\n\n```python\nclass Counter:\n    def __init__(self): self._n = 0; self._lock = asyncio.Lock()\n```\nRight for `TelegramFallback` (one singleton instance owns the state). The parent doc's recommendation is correct *here*.\n\n### Pattern B — Module-level lock (use when state is module-global)\n\n```python\n# src/feature/_init.py\nimport asyncio\n_state: dict = {}\n_state_lock = asyncio.Lock()   # lives with the module-global _state\n\nasync def cache_once(key, factory):\n    async with _state_lock:\n        if key not in _state:\n            _state[key] = await factory()\n        return _state[key]\n```\nThis is the *right* pattern when the state is genuinely a module global. The parent doc's cons (\"unclear ownership, hard to test\") are really just \"keep the lock next to the state\" — which Pattern B does.\n\n### Pattern C — App-state lock via FastAPI lifespan (preferred for the serving loop)\n\nThe cleanest place for a process-wide lock is on `app.state`, initialized in the **lifespan** handler so it is bound to the serving loop:\n\n```python\nfrom contextlib import asynccontextmanager\nfrom fastapi import FastAPI, Request\nimport asyncio\n\n@asynccontextmanager\nasync def lifespan(app: FastAPI):\n    app.state.dispatch_lock = asyncio.Lock()  # created on the serving loop\n    yield\n    # cleanup\n\napp = FastAPI(lifespan=lifespan)\n\n@app.post(\"/dispatch\")\nasync def dispatch(req: Request):\n    async with req.app.state.dispatch_lock:\n        ...\n```\nThis avoids the two classic module-level bugs: (1) the lock being created at import time before any loop exists, and (2) tests reusing a lock across `asyncio.run()` calls. See [FastAPI patterns](#fastapi-specific-concurrency-patterns).\n\n### The lazy-singleton nuance (relevant to this codebase)\n\n`get_store()` and `get_broadcaster()` use a classic lazy singleton:\n\n```python\n_store: SessionStore | None = None\ndef get_store(db_path=None) -> SessionStore:\n    global _store\n    if _store is None:\n        _store = SessionStore(db_path)   # no await inside\n    return _store\n```\n\nThis is **safe as written** on a single loop *because the constructor does not `await`* — control never yields between the `is None` check and the assignment, so no other coroutine can interleave. **It would become unsafe** the moment init becomes async (e.g. `await store.initialize()` inside the `if`): two coroutines could both see `None`, both start init, and one store would be discarded. The fix is either (a) initialize in lifespan, or (b) guard with a lock whose `acquire()` is the first `await`. Note this is a *single-loop* argument — under multiple workers each process correctly gets its own singleton.\n\n---\n\n## Mechanism 4: `threading.Lock` + `run_in_threadpool` (when you must)\n\n`threading.Lock` is the wrong default in async code (blocking acquire stalls the loop), but it is **required** when shared state is touched from a **threadpool** — i.e. from `def` routes, `fastapi.concurrency.run_in_threadpool`, `anyio.to_thread.run_sync`, or sync libraries that spin their own threads.\n\n### Blocking-acquire footgun\n\n```python\nself._lock = threading.Lock()\nasync def handler(self):\n    with self._lock:        # ❌ BLOCKS THE EVENT LOOP if contended\n        ...\n```\nEven an uncontended `threading.Lock` acquire is cheap, but a *contended* one parks the whole event loop thread — every other request stalls. This is the core reason the parent doc rejects `threading.Lock`, and it's correct for pure-async code.\n\n### Legitimate use: sync critical sections on the threadpool\n\nIf a function genuinely runs on a worker thread (blocking DB driver, sync CPU work), use `threading.Lock` there — it is the only lock those threads share:\n\n```python\nimport threading\n_lock = threading.Lock()\n\ndef _sync_compute():           # called via run_in_threadpool\n    with _lock:\n        ...                     # safe across threadpool threads\n```\n\n### The worst footgun: holding a `threading.Lock` across an `await`\n\n```python\nasync def broken(self):\n    with self._tlock:           # threading.Lock, no async release\n        await self._io()        # ❌ blocks the loop *and* can deadlock\n```\nA `threading.Lock` cannot be released by the event loop scheduler. Never hold one across an `await`. If you need a critical section that spans `await`s, that section is on the event loop — use `asyncio.Lock`.\n\n**Verdict:** `threading.Lock` only inside genuinely threaded code paths; never as the guard for an `async def` critical section.\n\n---\n\n## Mechanism 5: Atomic operations\n\n> **This is the section the parent doc (\"Option 4: Atomic Operations — NOT POSSIBLE\") gets wrong.** Python has no CAS for *arbitrary in-memory objects*, but it has robust atomicity at the **GIL level, the SQLite level, and the filesystem level**. For any state that is persisted, these are usually a better choice than a lock.\n\n### 5a. GIL-level atomicity (in-memory, thread layer only)\n\nUnder CPython's GIL, **a single bytecode** executes atomically with respect to other Python threads. Operations that compile to a single bytecode are atomic; **read-modify-write sequences are not**, even when they look like \"one operation\":\n\n| Operation | Bytecodes | Atomic? |\n|---|---|---|\n| `obj.flag = True` | `STORE_ATTR` | ✅ atomic |\n| `obj.n = 5` | `STORE_ATTR` | ✅ atomic |\n| `d[k] = v` | `STORE_SUBSCR` | ✅ atomic |\n| `lst.append(x)` | C-level call, holds GIL | ✅ atomic |\n| `lst[i] += 1` (single elem) | depends | ⚠️ usually, not guaranteed |\n| `obj.n += 1` | LOAD, ADD, STORE (3 bytecodes) | ❌ **NOT atomic** |\n| `if not obj.flag: obj.flag = True` | check + store | ❌ **NOT atomic** (the exact first-failure race) |\n| `obj.n = obj.n + 1` | same as `+=` | ❌ NOT atomic |\n\nSo a plain boolean flag set with `self.flag = True` is safe to **write** from a thread (the store is atomic), but the **check-then-set** pattern (`if not flag: flag = True`) is *not* atomic — exactly Race Condition #1 in the parent doc. The GIL is why `asyncio.Lock` is \"enough\" for single-worker async, but it is not a substitute for a lock or atomic op when a read-modify-write is involved.\n\n> Reproducibility note: I tried to demonstrate a lost update on `n += 1` with 8 threads × 500k iterations and could not reproduce it on CPython 3.12 in a tight loop — the GIL switch interval let each thread run long bursts. The behavior is still **not guaranteed** (the CPython docs explicitly disclaim atomicity of compound assignments), so don't rely on it. Non-reproducibility ≠ correctness.\n\n### 5b. SQLite atomicity (the codebase's actual shared state)\n\n`src/session/store.py` already declares `PRAGMA journal_mode=WAL` and `PRAGMA synchronous=NORMAL`. This gives strong, real atomicity — and it is the correct mechanism for any shared state that lives in `session.db`. Key techniques:\n\n**Transaction modes** (verified working on CPython 3.12's bundled sqlite3):\n\n| Mode | Lock acquired | Blocks | Use when |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| `BEGIN DEFERRED` (default) | none until first write, then RESERVED | readers never block; writers contend lazily | Most writes; cheap but two writers can collide → `SQLITE_BUSY` |\n| `BEGIN IMMEDIATE` | RESERVED immediately | other writers wait; readers proceed | Known write transaction; serializes writers cleanly |\n| `BEGIN EXCLUSIVE` | EXCLUSIVE | readers *and* writers wait | Migration / batch rewrite needing total isolation |\n\n**Atomic check-and-set / compare-and-swap via `UPDATE ... WHERE` + `rowcount`** (verified):\n\n```python\nasync with aiosqlite.connect(db_path) as db:\n    cur = await db.execute(\n        \"UPDATE first_failure SET claimed=1 WHERE id=1 AND claimed=0\"\n    )\n    await db.commit()\n    if cur.rowcount == 1:          # I won the race — exactly one writer sees this\n        log_first_failure()\n    # else: somebody else already claimed it; I log at DEBUG\n```\nThe `WHERE claimed=0` makes the check-and-set a **single atomic statement**. `cur.rowcount` tells you whether you were the winner — this is a real CAS primitive, expressed in SQL. No `asyncio.Lock` needed, and it is correct across workers and restarts.\n\n**Idempotent inserts** already used in this codebase:\n- `INSERT OR IGNORE` — `create_session`, `link_intent_to_topic` (no-op if the PK / unique already exists).\n- `INSERT OR REPLACE` — `set_topic_context` (clobber-and-replace is atomic).\n- `UPDATE ... SET v = v + 1` — atomic increment inside one statement (the DB engine holds the row lock for the duration; no lost update, unlike Python `+=`).\n\n**Concrete codebase race this fixes.** `SessionStore.find_or_create_topic()` (`store.py:444`) has a classic TOCTOU bug — it does a `SELECT` in one connection, then a separate `INSERT` in another, with an `await` in between:\n\n```python\nasync with aiosqlite.connect(self.db_path) as db:\n    ...  # SELECT: no row found\n# ← another concurrent call can land here, also see \"no row\"\ntopic_id = await self.create_topic(...)   # both create → duplicate topic\n```\nThere is no unique constraint on `(label, session_id)`, so two concurrent dispatches for the same new topic can each create a topic row. The atomic fix is a **unique index + `INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING ... RETURNING`** (SQLite ≥ 3.24; bundled sqlite3 on this box is 3.12-era so it supports it):\n\n```sql\nCREATE UNIQUE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS uq_topic_session_label\n  ON topics(label, session_id) WHERE scope='session' AND archived_at IS NULL;\n```\n```python\nasync with aiosqlite.connect(self.db_path) as db:\n    cur = await db.execute(\n        \"\"\"INSERT INTO topics (id, label, scope, session_id, created_at, last_active)\n           VALUES (?,?,?,?,?,?)\n           ON CONFLICT(label, session_id) DO NOTHING\n           RETURNING id\"\"\",\n        (tid, label, \"session\", session_id, now, now),\n    )\n    row = await cur.fetchone()\n    if row:           # we created it\n        return row[0], True\n    # else it already existed — fetch it\n    ...\n```\nOne statement, no lock, correct under all three concurrency layers. This is the single most useful pattern in the codebase.\n\n### 5c. Atomic file writes (`os.replace`)\n\nWhen the \"store\" is a file (config snapshots, the `plan.md` / notes, a JSON cache on disk), the atomic pattern is **write to a temp file in the same directory, then `os.replace`** (verified atomic on POSIX and Windows for same-filesystem renames):\n\n```python\nimport os, tempfile\ndef atomic_write(path: str, data: bytes) -> None:\n    fd, tmp = tempfile.mkstemp(dir=os.path.dirname(path), prefix=\".tmp-\")\n    try:\n        with os.fdopen(fd, \"wb\") as f:\n            f.write(data); f.flush(); os.fsync(f.fileno())\n        os.replace(tmp, path)        # atomic rename — readers never see a partial file\n    except BaseException:\n        os.unlink(tmp); raise\n```\n`os.replace` overwrites atomically; a crash leaves either the old file or the new file, never a half-written one. (Same filesystem required — `os.replace` across filesystems falls back to copy+delete and is not atomic.)\n\n---\n\n## FastAPI-specific concurrency patterns\n\n### `async def` vs `def` endpoints — the implicit threadpool\n\nFastAPI runs `async def` endpoints **directly on the event loop**; `def` endpoints are dispatched to a **threadpool** (Starlette's `anyio` worker, default 40 tokens). This is the single most important FastAPI concurrency fact:\n\n- A `def` endpoint touching shared in-memory state races against **threads**, not coroutines. `asyncio.Lock` does **not** protect it — it can't even be acquired from a sync function.\n- If the same shared object is touched by both an `async def` route and a `def` route (or a `BackgroundTask` that is a sync function), you have **mixed concurrency layers** and must use an atomic operation (DB) or a `threading.Lock` for the thread side — `asyncio.Lock` silently covers only half the access.\n\n**Implication for adc:** all the first-failure / persistence paths are `async def` and single-worker, so `asyncio.Lock` is *locally* sufficient — but only as long as those constraints hold. Persisting the state atomically in SQLite (5b) is more robust and removes the constraint entirely.\n\n### `run_in_threadpool` / `anyio.to_thread.run_sync`\n\nFor blocking calls inside `async def` (sync `httpx`, `requests`, blocking file/CPU work):\n\n```python\nfrom fastapi.concurrency import run_in_threadpool\nresult = await run_in_threadpool(blocking_fn, arg1, arg2)\n```\nThe function runs on a worker thread — shared state it touches needs thread-layer synchronization (5a/5b/`threading.Lock`), not `asyncio.Lock`.\n\n### Lifespan handlers — the right place to create locks\n\nCreate process-wide locks and singletons in `lifespan`, not at module import. They are then bound to the serving loop and initialized exactly once (replacing the fragile lazy-singleton pattern):\n\n```python\n@asynccontextmanager\nasync def lifespan(app: FastAPI):\n    app.state.store = await init_store()\n    app.state.telegram = TelegramFallback()      # its asyncio.Lock binds to this loop\n    app.state.dispatch_lock = asyncio.Lock()\n    yield\n    await app.state.store.close()\n```\n\n### Injecting locks via `Depends` / `app.state`\n\nRather than a module global, expose the lock through `request.app.state` or a dependency so it is testable and its lifetime is tied to the app:\n\n```python\ndef get_dispatch_lock(request: Request) -> asyncio.Lock:\n    return request.app.state.dispatch_lock\n\n@app.post(\"/dispatch\")\nasync def dispatch(_lock: asyncio.Lock = Depends(get_dispatch_lock)):\n    async with _lock: ...\n```\n\n### `BackgroundTasks` vs `asyncio.create_task`\n\n- FastAPI `BackgroundTasks` run *after the response is sent*; a sync task runs on the threadpool, an async task on the loop. Shared-state rules above apply.\n- `asyncio.create_task` runs on the loop immediately and concurrently with the handler. Anything both touch needs an `asyncio.Lock` (loop layer) or an atomic op.\n\n### Multi-worker deployment — the hard boundary\n\n`uvicorn --workers N` / `gunicorn -w N` runs N independent processes. **No in-memory lock, `asyncio` or `threading`, crosses this boundary.** If adc is ever scaled to multiple workers (currently it is single-process: `uvicorn src.main:app`), every in-memory lock silently stops coordinating. The only mechanisms that survive are the **atomic operations** in 5b (SQLite) or 5c (files), or an external coordinator (Redis `SETNX`, Postgres advisory locks). Designing persisted state around SQLite atomics from the start means the move to multi-worker is free.\n\n---\n\n## Decision matrix\n\n| State lives… | Concurrency layer(s) touching it | Recommended mechanism |\n|---|---|---|\n| In-memory, async-only (`TelegramFallback` flag) | Event loop | `asyncio.Lock` (instance or `app.state`) — *or* persist to DB if you want durability |\n| In-memory, touched by `def` routes / threadpool | Threads | `threading.Lock` for the threaded section; or move state into SQLite |\n| Module-global ephemeral cache | Event loop | Module-level `asyncio.Lock` next to the data, or lifespan `app.state` lock |\n| In SQLite (`session.db`) | Any / multi-worker | **SQLite atomicity**: `BEGIN IMMEDIATE`, `UPDATE…WHERE`+rowcount, `INSERT OR IGNORE`/`ON CONFLICT`. *No Python lock needed.* |\n| On disk (files) | Any / multi-worker | **`os.replace` temp-then-rename** (5c) |\n| Rate limiting / distributed | Multi-process / multi-host | External: Redis `SET NX EX`, or Postgres advisory lock |\n\n### For adc-50ld's first-failure state specifically\n\n- If the requirement is strictly \"one WARNING per process lifetime, in-memory only\" → `asyncio.Lock` on the singleton (the parent doc's recommendation is correct and minimal).\n- If the requirement is \"one WARNING per *deployment* across restarts/workers, with an accurate failure count\" → store `first_failure_at` / `failure_count` as rows in SQLite and use `UPDATE…WHERE claimed=0` + `rowcount` (5b). Durable, multi-worker-safe, and no loop blocking — strictly better, at the cost of one extra column/table.\n\n### For the SessionStore\n\n- Keep WAL + `synchronous=NORMAL` (correct as-is).\n- Fix the `find_or_create_topic` TOCTOU with a unique index + `ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING … RETURNING` (5b) — this is a real latent bug, not theoretical.\n- The `get_store()` / `get_broadcaster()` lazy singletons are safe *because* their constructors don't `await`; keep them that way or move init to lifespan.\n\n---\n\n## Appendix: verified behavior (CPython 3.12.12, this box)\n\nRan a probe script to confirm the non-obvious claims rather than rely on memory:\n\n| Claim | Result |\n|---|---|\n| `asyncio.Lock` is non-reentrant | ✅ Confirmed — second acquire in same task deadlocks (timed out via `wait_for`) |\n| Reusing a lock created in one `asyncio.run()` inside another | ✅ Works on 3.12 (no `loop` binding error) — though still create on the serving loop |\n| `+=` on a shared int loses updates | ⚠️ Not reproduced in a tight loop on 3.12 (GIL burst scheduling), but **not guaranteed atomic** — documented as such |\n| `BEGIN IMMEDIATE` / `BEGIN EXCLUSIVE` accepted | ✅ Both work |\n| `UPDATE … WHERE cond` as CAS via `rowcount` | ✅ First call `rowcount=1`, second `rowcount=0` |\n| `os.replace` atomic rename | ✅ Final present, temp gone |\n\n---\n\n## References\n\n- CPython docs — `asyncio` synchronization primitives: `Lock`, `Semaphore`, `Event`, `Condition`, `Queue`\n- CPython FAQ on thread state / GIL atomicity (single-bytecode atomicity; compound ops not guaranteed)\n- SQLite docs — transaction modes (`DEFERRED`/`IMMEDIATE`/`EXCLUSIVE`), WAL mode, `ON CONFLICT` upsert (≥3.24), `RETURNING` (≥3.35)\n- aiosqlite — async wrapper over sqlite3; one connection per `async with`, so transactions must stay within a single `connect()` block\n- FastAPI / Starlette — `async def` runs on the loop, `def` runs on the anyio threadpool (default 40 tokens); `run_in_threadpool`; lifespan handlers\n- Related design docs in this repo: `notes/adc-50ld-thread-safety-approach.md` (parent), `notes/adc-4ol5-race-conditions.md`, `notes/adc-2duz-state-storage-design.md`, `notes/adc-65l3-first-failure-state-structure.md`\n","numLines":429,"startLine":1,"totalLines":429}}}
