{"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"call_ddfd6fc2d7a84864b7c8d7f3","type":"tool_result","content":"1\t# Race Conditions for Concurrent First-Failure State in Async FastAPI\n2\t\n3\t## Overview\n4\t\n5\tThis document enumerates all possible race conditions when multiple async requests attempt to read/write first-failure state concurrently in the aide-de-camp FastAPI application. It covers specific race scenarios, state transitions that can conflict, and identifies which operations are atomic.\n6\t\n7\t## Context: Async FastAPI Execution Model\n8\t\n9\tFastAPI uses `asyncio` for concurrent request handling. Multiple coroutines can execute concurrently on a single thread via cooperative multitasking. This means:\n10\t\n11\t- **No preemption**: Coroutines yield control only at `await` points\n12\t- **Concurrent execution**: Multiple coroutines can be \"in flight\" simultaneously\n13\t- **Shared state**: All requests access the same singleton `TelegramFallback` instance\n14\t- **No locks by default**: Python does not provide atomic operations for object mutations\n15\t\n16\t**Critical insight**: Between the check (`if not state.has_failed`) and the act (`state.has_failed = True`), another coroutine can run and interleave its operations, causing race conditions.\n17\t\n18\t---\n19\t\n20\t## State Structure at Risk\n21\t\n22\t```python\n23\t# First-failure state (instance variables in TelegramFallback)\n24\tself._has_logged_first_failure: bool = False\n25\tself._failure_count: int = 0\n26\tself._first_failure_timestamp: Optional[datetime] = None\n27\tself._last_failure_timestamp: Optional[datetime] = None\n28\tself._notification_sent: bool = False\n29\tself._notification_sent_at: Optional[datetime] = None\n30\tself._channel_id: Optional[str] = None\n31\tself._error_type: Optional[str] = None\n32\tself._error_message: Optional[str] = None\n33\t```\n34\t\n35\tAll these fields are **mutable** and **shared** across concurrent requests. Without synchronization, multiple coroutines can read and write them simultaneously, leading to inconsistent state.\n36\t\n37\t---\n38\t\n39\t## Race Condition Categories\n40\t\n41\t### Category 1: Check-Then-Act Races on `has_logged_first_failure`\n42\t\n43\t#### Race 1.1: Concurrent First Failures (Duplicate WARNING Logs)\n44\t\n45\t**Scenario**: Multiple requests fail simultaneously at application startup.\n46\t\n47\t**Without Lock**:\n48\t```\n49\tTime  T0: Coroutine A checks has_logged_first_failure → False\n50\tTime  T1: Coroutine B checks has_logged_first_failure → False\n51\tTime  T2: Coroutine A sets has_logged_first_failure = True, logs WARNING\n52\tTime  T3: Coroutine B sets has_logged_first_failure = True, logs WARNING ❌ DUPLICATE\n53\t```\n54\t\n55\t**Impact**:\n56\t- Multiple WARNING logs for the same \"first\" failure\n57\t- Violates requirement: exactly one WARNING per startup\n58\t- Log spam when bridge is down\n59\t- May trigger multiple alert notifications\n60\t\n61\t**State After Race**:\n62\t```\n63\thas_logged_first_failure = True  (correct)\n64\tfailure_count = 2                 (correct)\n65\tfirst_failure_timestamp = T2      (one of the two, non-deterministic)\n66\t```\n67\t\n68\t**With `asyncio.Lock`**:\n69\t```\n70\tTime  T0: Coroutine A acquires lock\n71\tTime  T1: Coroutine B blocks on lock (queued)\n72\tTime  T2: Coroutine A checks → False, sets → True, logs WARNING, releases lock\n73\tTime  T3: Coroutine B acquires lock, checks → True, logs DEBUG, releases lock ✅\n74\t```\n75\t\n76\t**Result**: Exactly one WARNING, N-1 DEBUG logs.\n77\t\n78\t---\n79\t\n80\t#### Race 1.2: Read-During-Write on `has_logged_first_failure`\n81\t\n82\t**Scenario**: A status read (`get_bridge_status()`) occurs while another coroutine is writing the first-failure state.\n83\t\n84\t**Without Lock**:\n85\t```\n86\tTime  T0: Coroutine A (failure handler) checks has_logged_first_failure → False\n87\tTime  T1: Coroutine B (status endpoint) reads has_logged_first_failure → False\n88\tTime  T2: Coroutine B reads first_failure_timestamp → None\n89\tTime  T3: Coroutine A sets has_logged_first_failure = True\n90\tTime  T4: Coroutine A sets first_failure_timestamp = now\n91\tTime  T5: Coroutine B returns status: {\"has_logged_first_failure\": False, \"first_failure_timestamp\": None}\n92\t```\n93\t\n94\t**Impact**:\n95\t- Status endpoint returns stale data (inconsistent view)\n96\t- Monitoring/metrics miss the first-failure event\n97\t- Health checks show \"healthy\" despite failure in progress\n98\t\n99\t**State After Race**:\n100\t```\n101\thas_logged_first_failure = True  (correct)\n102\tfirst_failure_timestamp = T4     (correct)\n103\tStatus returned: False, None      (stale)\n104\t```\n105\t\n106\t**With `asyncio.Lock`** (write path only):\n107\t- Reads do NOT acquire lock (by design)\n108\t- Reads may see intermediate state, but this is acceptable for monitoring\n109\t- If strict consistency is required, reads must also acquire lock\n110\t\n111\t**Verdict**: Acceptable inconsistency for monitoring; critical operations require lock.\n112\t\n113\t---\n114\t\n115\t### Category 2: Lost Update Races on Counters\n116\t\n117\t#### Race 2.1: Concurrent Increment of `failure_count`\n118\t\n119\t**Scenario**: Multiple failures increment the counter simultaneously.\n120\t\n121\t**Without Lock**:\n122\t```\n123\tTime  T0: failure_count = 5\n124\tTime  T1: Coroutine A reads failure_count → 5\n125\tTime  T2: Coroutine B reads failure_count → 5\n126\tTime  T3: Coroutine A increments: failure_count = 5 + 1 = 6, writes\n127\tTime  T4: Coroutine B increments: failure_count = 5 + 1 = 6, writes ❌ LOST UPDATE\n128\tTime  T5: failure_count = 6  (should be 7)\n129\t```\n130\t\n131\t**Impact**:\n132\t- Inaccurate failure count\n133\t- Misleading diagnostics\n134\t- Wrong metrics for monitoring\n135\t- Underestimates failure frequency\n136\t\n137\t**State After Race**:\n138\t```\n139\tfailure_count = 6  (incorrect, should be 7)\n140\t```\n141\t\n142\t**With `asyncio.Lock`**:\n143\t```\n144\tTime  T0: Coroutine A acquires lock\n145\tTime  T1: Coroutine B blocks on lock\n146\tTime  T2: Coroutine A reads (5), increments (6), writes, releases lock\n147\tTime  T3: Coroutine B acquires lock, reads (6), increments (7), writes, releases lock ✅\n148\t```\n149\t\n150\t**Result**: All updates serialized, count is accurate.\n151\t\n152\t---\n153\t\n154\t#### Race 2.2: Increment-During-Read on `failure_count`\n155\t\n156\t**Scenario**: Status endpoint reads `failure_count` while it's being incremented.\n157\t\n158\t**Without Lock**:\n159\t```\n160\tTime  T0: failure_count = 10\n161\tTime  T1: Coroutine A (status) reads failure_count → 10\n162\tTime  T2: Coroutine B (failure handler) reads failure_count → 10\n163\tTime  T3: Coroutine B increments: failure_count = 11\n164\tTime  T4: Coroutine A returns status: {\"failure_count\": 10}  (stale)\n165\t```\n166\t\n167\t**Impact**:\n168\t- Status shows slightly stale count (acceptable for monitoring)\n169\t- No correctness issue (count is monotonic, stale value is just outdated)\n170\t\n171\t**Verdict**: Acceptable for monitoring; no lock needed for reads.\n172\t\n173\t---\n174\t\n175\t### Category 3: Timestamp Assignment Races\n176\t\n177\t#### Race 3.1: Concurrent Write of `first_failure_timestamp`\n178\t\n179\t**Scenario**: Multiple failures attempt to set `first_failure_timestamp` simultaneously.\n180\t\n181\t**Without Lock**:\n182\t```\n183\tTime  T0: first_failure_timestamp = None\n184\tTime  T1: Coroutine A checks first_failure_timestamp → None\n185\tTime  T2: Coroutine B checks first_failure_timestamp → None\n186\tTime  T3: Coroutine A sets first_failure_timestamp = datetime_A\n187\tTime  T4: Coroutine B sets first_failure_timestamp = datetime_B ❌ OVERWRITE\n188\tTime  T5: first_failure_timestamp = datetime_B  (non-deterministic)\n189\t```\n190\t\n191\t**Impact**:\n192\t- Lost first-failure timestamp accuracy\n193\t- Difficult to correlate with logs\n194\t- Misleading diagnostics (which was really first?)\n195\t- Non-deterministic behavior\n196\t\n197\t**State After Race**:\n198\t```\n199\tfirst_failure_timestamp = datetime_B  (one of the two, non-deterministic)\n200\t```\n201\t\n202\t**With `asyncio.Lock`**:\n203\t```\n204\tTime  T0: Coroutine A acquires lock\n205\tTime  T1: Coroutine B blocks on lock\n206\tTime  T2: Coroutine A checks → None, sets → datetime_A, releases lock\n207\tTime  T3: Coroutine B acquires lock, checks → datetime_A (not None), skips set, releases lock ✅\n208\t```\n209\t\n210\t**Result**: First timestamp is preserved exactly once.\n211\t\n212\t---\n213\t\n214\t#### Race 3.2: Write-During-Read on Timestamps\n215\t\n216\t**Scenario**: Status endpoint reads `first_failure_timestamp` while it's being written.\n217\t\n218\t**Without Lock**:\n219\t```\n220\tTime  T0: first_failure_timestamp = None\n221\tTime  T1: Coroutine A (status) reads first_failure_timestamp → None\n222\tTime  T2: Coroutine B (failure handler) sets first_failure_timestamp = now\n223\tTime  T3: Coroutine A returns status: {\"first_failure_timestamp\": None}  (stale)\n224\t```\n225\t\n226\t**Impact**:\n227\t- Status shows stale timestamp (acceptable for monitoring)\n228\t- No correctness issue (timestamp was None at read time)\n229\t\n230\t**Verdict**: Acceptable for monitoring.\n231\t\n232\t---\n233\t\n234\t### Category 4: Multi-Field Consistency Races\n235\t\n236\t#### Race 4.1: Inconsistent Snapshot of State\n237\t\n238\t**Scenario**: Multiple fields are updated as a group, but a read observes a partial update.\n239\t\n240\t**Without Lock**:\n241\t```\n242\tTime  T0: has_logged_first_failure = False, first_failure_timestamp = None, failure_count = 0\n243\tTime  T1: Coroutine A (status) reads has_logged_first_failure → False\n244\tTime  T2: Coroutine B (failure handler) sets has_logged_first_failure = True\n245\tTime  T3: Coroutine B sets first_failure_timestamp = now_A\n246\tTime  T4: Coroutine B sets failure_count = 1\n247\tTime  T5: Coroutine A reads first_failure_timestamp → now_A\n248\tTime  T6: Coroutine A reads failure_count → 1\n249\tTime  T7: Coroutine A returns: {\"has_logged_first_failure\": False, \"first_failure_timestamp\": now_A, \"failure_count\": 1} ❌ INCONSISTENT\n250\t```\n251\t\n252\t**Impact**:\n253\t- Status shows inconsistent state (flag=False but timestamp=now_A)\n254\t- Confusing diagnostics (was there a failure or not?)\n255\t- Breaks assumptions about field relationships\n256\t\n257\t**State After Race**:\n258\t```\n259\thas_logged_first_failure = True\n260\tfirst_failure_timestamp = now_A\n261\tfailure_count = 1\n262\tStatus returned: False, now_A, 1  (inconsistent combination)\n263\t```\n264\t\n265\t**With `asyncio.Lock`** (write path only):\n266\t- Writes are serialized (all fields updated atomically under lock)\n267\t- Reads may still see intermediate state (acceptable for monitoring)\n268\t- If strict consistency is required, reads must also acquire lock\n269\t\n270\t**Verdict**: Acceptable for monitoring; critical decision logic requires lock.\n271\t\n272\t---\n273\t\n274\t#### Race 4.2: Notification Sent Flag Inconsistency\n275\t\n276\t**Scenario**: `notification_sent` flag and `notification_sent_at` timestamp are updated separately.\n277\t\n278\t**Without Lock**:\n279\t```\n280\tTime  T0: notification_sent = False, notification_sent_at = None\n281\tTime  T1: Coroutine A (status) reads notification_sent → False\n282\tTime  T2: Coroutine B (failure handler) sets notification_sent = True\n283\tTime  T3: Coroutine B sets notification_sent_at = now_B\n284\tTime  T4: Coroutine A reads notification_sent_at → now_B\n285\tTime  T5: Coroutine A returns: {\"notification_sent\": False, \"notification_sent_at\": now_B} ❌ INCONSISTENT\n286\t```\n287\t\n288\t**Impact**:\n289\t- Status shows \"not sent\" but has a timestamp (contradiction)\n290\t- Breaks assumptions about flag/timestamp relationship\n291\t- Confusing diagnostics\n292\t\n293\t**With `asyncio.Lock`**:\n294\t- All fields updated atomically under lock\n295\t- Consistent snapshot guaranteed\n296\t\n297\t---\n298\t\n299\t### Category 5: Check-Then-Act on Reset Operations\n300\t\n301\t#### Race 5.1: Reset-During-Failure Handling\n302\t\n303\t**Scenario**: Admin resets state while failures are still being processed.\n304\t\n305\t**Without Lock**:\n306\t```\n307\tTime  T0: has_logged_first_failure = True, failure_count = 10\n308\tTime  T1: Coroutine A (admin reset) checks has_logged_first_failure → True\n309\tTime  T2: Coroutine B (failure handler) checks has_logged_first_failure → True\n310\tTime  T3: Coroutine A sets has_logged_first_failure = False (reset)\n311\tTime  T4: Coroutine A sets failure_count = 0 (reset)\n312\tTime  T5: Coroutine B logs DEBUG (sees flag was True, now False) ❌ CONFUSING\n313\tTime  T6: Coroutine B increments failure_count = 1 (based on stale check)\n314\t```\n315\t\n316\t**Impact**:\n317\t- Reset is partially undone by concurrent failure handler\n318\t- State is inconsistent (flag=False, but failure_count was 10, now 1)\n319\t- Confusing behavior\n320\t\n321\t**With `asyncio.Lock`**:\n322\t- Reset and failure handling serialize\n323\t- Either reset completes first, then failure handles new state\n324\t- Or failure completes first, then reset clears it\n325\t- Both produce consistent final state\n326\t\n327\t---\n328\t\n329\t#### Race 5.2: Concurrent Reset Calls\n330\t\n331\t**Scenario**: Two admins trigger reset simultaneously.\n332\t\n333\t**Without Lock**:\n334\t```\n335\tTime  T0: has_logged_first_failure = True, failure_count = 10\n336\tTime  T1: Coroutine A (admin reset 1) reads has_logged_first_failure → True\n337\tTime  T2: Coroutine B (admin reset 2) reads has_logged_first_failure → True\n338\tTime  T3: Coroutine A sets has_logged_first_failure = False\n339\tTime  T4: Coroutine A sets failure_count = 0\n340\tTime  T5: Coroutine B sets has_logged_first_failure = False\n341\tTime  T6: Coroutine B sets failure_count = 0  (redundant but harmless)\n342\t```\n343\t\n344\t**Impact**:\n345\t- Redundant resets (harmless if all fields are set to defaults)\n346\t- No correctness issue, just inefficiency\n347\t\n348\t**Verdict**: Acceptable; redundant resets are idempotent.\n349\t\n350\t---\n351\t\n352\t### Category 6: Complex Multi-Step Operation Races\n353\t\n354\t#### Race 6.1: Failure Recording + Notification Sending\n355\t\n356\t**Scenario**: Recording first failure and sending notification is a multi-step operation.\n357\t\n358\t**Without Lock**:\n359\t```\n360\tTime  T0: has_logged_first_failure = False, notification_sent = False\n361\tTime  T1: Coroutine A checks has_logged_first_failure → False\n362\tTime  T2: Coroutine B checks has_logged_first_failure → False\n363\tTime  T3: Coroutine A sets has_logged_first_failure = True\n364\tTime  T4: Coroutine A sends notification (async HTTP call)\n365\tTime  T5: Coroutine B sets has_logged_first_failure = True ❌ DUPLICATE\n366\tTime  T6: Coroutine B sends notification ❌ DUPLICATE\n367\t```\n368\t\n369\t**Impact**:\n370\t- Duplicate notifications sent\n371\t- State updated twice (redundant but harmless)\n372\t- User receives multiple alerts for same event\n373\t\n374\t**With `asyncio.Lock`**:\n375\t- Lock protects the entire \"check → update → notify\" sequence\n376\t- Only one coroutine executes the full sequence\n377\t- Others see `has_logged_first_failure = True` and skip notification\n378\t\n379\t**Verdict**: Critical race; lock is required.\n380\t\n381\t---\n382\t\n383\t#### Race 6.2: Failure Recording + Persistence (Database Write)\n384\t\n385\t**Scenario**: Recording first failure and persisting to database.\n386\t\n387\t**Without Lock**:\n388\t```\n389\tTime  T0: has_logged_first_failure = False\n390\tTime  T1: Coroutine A checks has_logged_first_failure → False\n391\tTime  T2: Coroutine B checks has_logged_first_failure → False\n392\tTime  T3: Coroutine A sets has_logged_first_failure = True\n393\tTime  T4: Coroutine A persists to DB (async I/O)\n394\tTime  T5: Coroutine B sets has_logged_first_failure = True ❌ DUPLICATE\n395\tTime  T6: Coroutine B persists to DB ❌ DUPLICATE\n396\t```\n397\t\n398\t**Impact**:\n399\t- Duplicate database records\n400\t- Wasted storage\n401\t- Inconsistent query results\n402\t\n403\t**With `asyncio.Lock`**:\n404\t- Lock protects check-and-set (state mutation)\n405\t- Persistence can happen outside lock (only if it's optional/slow)\n406\t- Or lock protects entire sequence if persistence is critical\n407\t\n408\t**Design Decision**: If persistence is optional (monitoring only), it can happen outside lock. If persistence is critical (audit trail), it must be inside lock.\n409\t\n410\t---\n411\t\n412\t## Atomic vs Non-Atomic Operations\n413\t\n414\t### Atomic Operations (No Lock Needed)\n415\t\n416\tThese operations are inherently atomic in Python due to the GIL or immutability:\n417\t\n418\t1. **Reading immutable types**: `bool`, `int`, `float` reads are atomic at the bytecode level\n419\t2. **Writing to a single variable**: Assignment to a simple variable is atomic\n420\t3. **Reading/writing `asyncio.Lock` state**: Lock acquisition/release is atomic\n421\t\n422\t**Caveat**: While individual operations are atomic, **sequences** (check-then-act) are not.\n423\t\n424\t---\n425\t\n426\t### Non-Atomic Operations (Lock Required)\n427\t\n428\tThese operations require `asyncio.Lock` for thread-safety:\n429\t\n430\t1. **Check-then-act on `has_logged_first_failure`**:\n431\t   ```python\n432\t   if not self._has_logged_first_failure:  # ❌ Not atomic\n433\t       self._has_logged_first_failure = True\n434\t   ```\n435\t\n436\t2. **Read-modify-write on `failure_count`**:\n437\t   ```python\n438\t   self._failure_count += 1  # ❌ Not atomic (read, add, write)\n439\t   ```\n440\t\n441\t3. **Multi-field updates**:\n442\t   ```python\n443\t   self._has_logged_first_failure = True\n444\t   self._first_failure_timestamp = now  # ❌ Not atomic together\n445\t   self._failure_count = 1\n446\t   ```\n447\t\n448\t4. **Conditional updates based on multiple fields**:\n449\t   ```python\n450\t   if not self._has_logged_first_failure and self._failure_count == 0:  # ❌ Not atomic\n451\t       # ...\n452\t   ```\n453\t\n454\t---\n455\t\n456\t## Critical Sections Requiring Lock Protection\n457\t\n458\t### Critical Section 1: First-Failure Detection\n459\t\n460\t```python\n461\tasync with self._first_failure_lock:\n462\t    if not self._has_logged_first_failure:\n463\t        # Entire block must be atomic\n464\t        self._has_logged_first_failure = True\n465\t        self._first_failure_timestamp = datetime.now(timezone.utc)\n466\t        self._failure_count = 1\n467\t        self._last_failure_timestamp = datetime.now(timezone.utc)\n468\t        logger.warning(\"First failure...\")\n469\t        await send_notification(...)  # If notification is critical\n470\t```\n471\t\n472\t**Why**: Check-then-act on flag, multi-field update, notification sending.\n473\t\n474\t---\n475\t\n476\t### Critical Section 2: Failure Count Increment\n477\t\n478\t```python\n479\tasync with self._first_failure_lock:\n480\t    self._failure_count += 1  # Read-modify-write\n481\t    self._last_failure_timestamp = datetime.now(timezone.utc)\n482\t```\n483\t\n484\t**Why**: Increment is read-modify-write, timestamp update must be consistent.\n485\t\n486\t---\n487\t\n488\t### Critical Section 3: State Reset\n489\t\n490\t```python\n491\tasync with self._first_failure_lock:\n492\t    self._has_logged_first_failure = False\n493\t    self._first_failure_timestamp = None\n494\t    self._notification_sent = False\n495\t    self._notification_sent_at = None\n496\t    # Keep failure_count for diagnostics\n497\t```\n498\t\n499\t**Why**: Multi-field reset must be atomic to avoid partial state.\n500\t\n501\t---\n502\t\n503\t### Non-Critical Sections (No Lock Required)\n504\t\n505\t1. **Status reads** (`get_bridge_status()`):\n506\t   ```python\n507\t   return {\n508\t       \"has_logged_first_failure\": self._has_logged_first_failure,\n509\t       \"failure_count\": self._failure_count,\n510\t       # ... (reads are atomic, slight staleness is acceptable)\n511\t   }\n512\t   ```\n513\t   **Why**: Reads are atomic; monitoring can tolerate slight staleness.\n514\t\n515\t2. **Logging operations**:\n516\t   ```python\n517\t   logger.debug(f\"Failure count: {self._failure_count}\")\n518\t   ```\n519\t   **Why**: Logging is side-effect-free for state.\n520\t\n521\t---\n522\t\n523\t## State Transition Conflicts\n524\t\n525\t### Conflict 1: First Failure vs Subsequent Failure\n526\t\n527\t**Transition**: `has_logged_first_failure: False → True`\n528\t\n529\t**Conflict**: Multiple concurrent failures race to be \"first\".\n530\t\n531\t**Resolution**: Lock ensures exactly one succeeds; others become \"subsequent\".\n532\t\n533\t---\n534\t\n535\t### Conflict 2: Subsequent Failure vs Reset\n536\t\n537\t**Transition**: `has_logged_first_failure: True → False` (reset)\n538\t\n539\t**Conflict**: Failure handler increments count while reset clears flag.\n540\t\n541\t**Resolution**: Lock serializes operations; reset happens either before or after increment.\n542\t\n543\t---\n544\t\n545\t### Conflict 3: Reset vs First Failure\n546\t\n547\t**Transition**: `has_logged_first_failure: False → True` (after reset)\n548\t\n549\t**Conflict**: Reset clears state while new failure sets it.\n550\t\n551\t**Resolution**: Lock serializes; whichever acquires lock first wins.\n552\t\n553\t---\n554\t\n555\t## Performance Implications of Locking\n556\t\n557\t### Lock Contention Analysis\n558\t\n559\t| Scenario | Lock Usage | Contention | Impact |\n560\t|----------|-----------|-----------|--------|\n561\t| Bridge healthy (no failures) | None | None | Zero ✅ |\n562\t| First failure at startup | Single acquisition | None | Negligible (~1-2ms) |\n563\t| Multiple concurrent failures | N acquisitions | Low-moderate | Acceptable (~2-5ms per request) |\n564\t| High traffic with bridge down | Many acquisitions | Moderate | Bounded (queue wait = N × 2ms) |\n565\t\n566\t**Worst Case**: 100 concurrent failures = 200ms total queue wait = 2ms average per request.\n567\t\n568\t**Why this is acceptable**:\n569\t- Bridge is already down (latency doesn't matter for failed sends)\n570\t- Sends are already failing (no \"happy path\" to optimize)\n571\t- Logging overhead dominates (2ms lock is <10% of total)\n572\t\n573\t---\n574\t\n575\t## Mitigation Strategy\n576\t\n577\t### Recommended: `asyncio.Lock` with Minimal Critical Section\n578\t\n579\t```python\n580\tclass TelegramFallback:\n581\t    def __init__(self):\n582\t        self._first_failure_lock = asyncio.Lock()\n583\t\n584\t    async def _handle_send_failure(self, error_context: str = \"\"):\n585\t        async with self._first_failure_lock:  # Serialize access\n586\t            if not self._has_logged_first_failure:\n587\t                # First failure - log at WARNING level\n588\t                logger.warning(\"First failure...\")\n589\t                self._has_logged_first_failure = True\n590\t                self._first_failure_timestamp = datetime.now(timezone.utc)\n591\t                self._failure_count = 1\n592\t            else:\n593\t                # Subsequent failure - log at DEBUG level\n594\t                logger.debug(f\"Repeated failure #{self._failure_count + 1}\")\n595\t                self._failure_count += 1\n596\t            self._last_failure_timestamp = datetime.now(timezone.utc)\n597\t```\n598\t\n599\t**Why this works**:\n600\t- Lock protects the entire check-and-set sequence\n601\t- Minimal overhead (lock held for microseconds)\n602\t- No I/O inside lock (logging is fast, no DB/network calls)\n603\t- Serializes the critical section only\n604\t\n605\t---\n606\t\n607\t## Testing Race Conditions\n608\t\n609\t### Test 1: Concurrent First Failures\n610\t\n611\t```python\n612\t@pytest.mark.asyncio\n613\tasync def test_concurrent_first_failures():\n614\t    \"\"\"Verify only one WARNING is logged when multiple requests fail concurrently.\"\"\"\n615\t    fallback = TelegramFallback()\n616\t    \n617\t    # Trigger 10 concurrent failures\n618\t    await asyncio.gather(*[\n619\t        fallback._handle_send_failure(f\"error{i}\")\n620\t        for i in range(10)\n621\t    ])\n622\t    \n623\t    # Verify state\n624\t    assert fallback._has_logged_first_failure == True\n625\t    assert fallback._failure_count == 10\n626\t    \n627\t    # Verify logs (count WARNING vs DEBUG)\n628\t    # Should see: 1 WARNING, 9 DEBUG\n629\t```\n630\t\n631\t### Test 2: Concurrent Increments\n632\t\n633\t```python\n634\t@pytest.mark.asyncio\n635\tasync def test_concurrent_increment_race():\n636\t    \"\"\"Verify failure_count is accurate under concurrent increments.\"\"\"\n637\t    fallback = TelegramFallback()\n638\t    \n639\t    # Simulate first failure\n640\t    await fallback._handle_send_failure(\"first\")\n641\t    \n642\t    # Clear flag to allow increment-only race\n643\t    fallback._has_logged_first_failure = True\n644\t    \n645\t    # Trigger 100 concurrent increments\n646\t    await asyncio.gather(*[\n647\t        fallback._handle_send_failure(f\"error{i}\")\n648\t        for i in range(100)\n649\t    ])\n650\t    \n651\t    # Verify no lost updates\n652\t    assert fallback._failure_count == 101  # 1 + 100\n653\t```\n654\t\n655\t### Test 3: Reset During Failures\n656\t\n657\t```python\n658\t@pytest.mark.asyncio\n659\tasync def test_reset_during_failures():\n660\t    \"\"\"Verify reset and failures serialize correctly.\"\"\"\n661\t    fallback = TelegramFallback()\n662\t    \n663\t    # Start with failures\n664\t    await fallback._handle_send_failure(\"first\")\n665\t    assert fallback._has_logged_first_failure == True\n666\t    \n667\t    # Concurrent reset and failure\n668\t    reset_task = asyncio.create_task(fallback.reset_first_failure_state())\n669\t    failure_task = asyncio.create_task(fallback._handle_send_failure(\"concurrent\"))\n670\t    \n671\t    await asyncio.gather(reset_task, failure_task)\n672\t    \n673\t    # State should be consistent (either reset happened or failure)\n674\t    assert (fallback._has_logged_first_failure == True or \n675\t            fallback._has_logged_first_failure == False)\n676\t```\n677\t\n678\t---\n679\t\n680\t## Summary\n681\t\n682\t### All Identified Race Conditions\n683\t\n684\t| Category | Race | Impact | Mitigation |\n685\t|----------|------|--------|------------|\n686\t| Check-then-act | Concurrent first failures | Duplicate WARNING logs | `asyncio.Lock` |\n687\t| Check-then-act | Read-during-write | Stale status data | Acceptable for monitoring |\n688\t| Lost update | Concurrent increment | Inaccurate count | `asyncio.Lock` |\n689\t| Lost update | Increment-during-read | Stale count | Acceptable for monitoring |\n690\t| Timestamp | Concurrent timestamp write | Non-deterministic timestamp | `asyncio.Lock` |\n691\t| Timestamp | Write-during-read | Stale timestamp | Acceptable for monitoring |\n692\t| Multi-field | Inconsistent snapshot | Contradictory state | `asyncio.Lock` for writes |\n693\t| Multi-field | Notification flag inconsistency | Contradictory state | `asyncio.Lock` |\n694\t| Reset | Reset-during-failure | Partial undo | `asyncio.Lock` |\n695\t| Reset | Concurrent reset | Redundant resets | Acceptable (idempotent) |\n696\t| Multi-step | Failure + notification | Duplicate notifications | `asyncio.Lock` |\n697\t| Multi-step | Failure + persistence | Duplicate DB records | `asyncio.Lock` (or outside if optional) |\n698\t\n699\t### Atomic Operations (No Lock)\n700\t\n701\t- Reading/writing single variables (bool, int)\n702\t- Reading immutable types\n703\t\n704\t### Non-Atomic Operations (Lock Required)\n705\t\n706\t- Check-then-act sequences\n707\t- Read-modify-write (increment)\n708\t- Multi-field updates\n709\t- Conditional updates based on multiple fields\n710\t\n711\t### Recommended Strategy\n712\t\n713\t1. **Use `asyncio.Lock`** for all state mutations (write operations)\n714\t2. **No lock for reads** (monitoring can tolerate slight staleness)\n715\t3. **Minimal critical section** (only state mutations, no I/O)\n716\t4. **Test with `asyncio.gather()`** to verify race condition prevention\n717\t\n718\t### Performance Impact\n719\t\n720\t- **Happy path**: Zero overhead (no lock acquisition)\n721\t- **Error path**: +2-5ms per request (negligible, only affects degraded state)\n722\t- **Worst case**: ~200ms total wait for 100 concurrent failures (acceptable)\n723\t\n724\t---\n725\t\n726\t**Document**: adc-4ol5  \n727\t**Status**: ✅ Complete  \n728\t**Dependencies**: adc-50ld (thread-safety approach), adc-65l3 (state structure), adc-2duz (storage design)  \n729\t**Date**: 2026-07-02\n730\t"}]},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"da5125d1-116e-4164-b804-5e12910ca7dd","uuid":"43e36f00-a3a4-4935-b8a2-1d0ed1a9d011","timestamp":"2026-07-19T21:35:40.539Z","tool_use_result":{"type":"text","file":{"filePath":"/home/coding/aide-de-camp/docs/race-conditions-first-failure-state.md","content":"# Race Conditions for Concurrent First-Failure State in Async FastAPI\n\n## Overview\n\nThis document enumerates all possible race conditions when multiple async requests attempt to read/write first-failure state concurrently in the aide-de-camp FastAPI application. It covers specific race scenarios, state transitions that can conflict, and identifies which operations are atomic.\n\n## Context: Async FastAPI Execution Model\n\nFastAPI uses `asyncio` for concurrent request handling. Multiple coroutines can execute concurrently on a single thread via cooperative multitasking. This means:\n\n- **No preemption**: Coroutines yield control only at `await` points\n- **Concurrent execution**: Multiple coroutines can be \"in flight\" simultaneously\n- **Shared state**: All requests access the same singleton `TelegramFallback` instance\n- **No locks by default**: Python does not provide atomic operations for object mutations\n\n**Critical insight**: Between the check (`if not state.has_failed`) and the act (`state.has_failed = True`), another coroutine can run and interleave its operations, causing race conditions.\n\n---\n\n## State Structure at Risk\n\n```python\n# First-failure state (instance variables in TelegramFallback)\nself._has_logged_first_failure: bool = False\nself._failure_count: int = 0\nself._first_failure_timestamp: Optional[datetime] = None\nself._last_failure_timestamp: Optional[datetime] = None\nself._notification_sent: bool = False\nself._notification_sent_at: Optional[datetime] = None\nself._channel_id: Optional[str] = None\nself._error_type: Optional[str] = None\nself._error_message: Optional[str] = None\n```\n\nAll these fields are **mutable** and **shared** across concurrent requests. Without synchronization, multiple coroutines can read and write them simultaneously, leading to inconsistent state.\n\n---\n\n## Race Condition Categories\n\n### Category 1: Check-Then-Act Races on `has_logged_first_failure`\n\n#### Race 1.1: Concurrent First Failures (Duplicate WARNING Logs)\n\n**Scenario**: Multiple requests fail simultaneously at application startup.\n\n**Without Lock**:\n```\nTime  T0: Coroutine A checks has_logged_first_failure → False\nTime  T1: Coroutine B checks has_logged_first_failure → False\nTime  T2: Coroutine A sets has_logged_first_failure = True, logs WARNING\nTime  T3: Coroutine B sets has_logged_first_failure = True, logs WARNING ❌ DUPLICATE\n```\n\n**Impact**:\n- Multiple WARNING logs for the same \"first\" failure\n- Violates requirement: exactly one WARNING per startup\n- Log spam when bridge is down\n- May trigger multiple alert notifications\n\n**State After Race**:\n```\nhas_logged_first_failure = True  (correct)\nfailure_count = 2                 (correct)\nfirst_failure_timestamp = T2      (one of the two, non-deterministic)\n```\n\n**With `asyncio.Lock`**:\n```\nTime  T0: Coroutine A acquires lock\nTime  T1: Coroutine B blocks on lock (queued)\nTime  T2: Coroutine A checks → False, sets → True, logs WARNING, releases lock\nTime  T3: Coroutine B acquires lock, checks → True, logs DEBUG, releases lock ✅\n```\n\n**Result**: Exactly one WARNING, N-1 DEBUG logs.\n\n---\n\n#### Race 1.2: Read-During-Write on `has_logged_first_failure`\n\n**Scenario**: A status read (`get_bridge_status()`) occurs while another coroutine is writing the first-failure state.\n\n**Without Lock**:\n```\nTime  T0: Coroutine A (failure handler) checks has_logged_first_failure → False\nTime  T1: Coroutine B (status endpoint) reads has_logged_first_failure → False\nTime  T2: Coroutine B reads first_failure_timestamp → None\nTime  T3: Coroutine A sets has_logged_first_failure = True\nTime  T4: Coroutine A sets first_failure_timestamp = now\nTime  T5: Coroutine B returns status: {\"has_logged_first_failure\": False, \"first_failure_timestamp\": None}\n```\n\n**Impact**:\n- Status endpoint returns stale data (inconsistent view)\n- Monitoring/metrics miss the first-failure event\n- Health checks show \"healthy\" despite failure in progress\n\n**State After Race**:\n```\nhas_logged_first_failure = True  (correct)\nfirst_failure_timestamp = T4     (correct)\nStatus returned: False, None      (stale)\n```\n\n**With `asyncio.Lock`** (write path only):\n- Reads do NOT acquire lock (by design)\n- Reads may see intermediate state, but this is acceptable for monitoring\n- If strict consistency is required, reads must also acquire lock\n\n**Verdict**: Acceptable inconsistency for monitoring; critical operations require lock.\n\n---\n\n### Category 2: Lost Update Races on Counters\n\n#### Race 2.1: Concurrent Increment of `failure_count`\n\n**Scenario**: Multiple failures increment the counter simultaneously.\n\n**Without Lock**:\n```\nTime  T0: failure_count = 5\nTime  T1: Coroutine A reads failure_count → 5\nTime  T2: Coroutine B reads failure_count → 5\nTime  T3: Coroutine A increments: failure_count = 5 + 1 = 6, writes\nTime  T4: Coroutine B increments: failure_count = 5 + 1 = 6, writes ❌ LOST UPDATE\nTime  T5: failure_count = 6  (should be 7)\n```\n\n**Impact**:\n- Inaccurate failure count\n- Misleading diagnostics\n- Wrong metrics for monitoring\n- Underestimates failure frequency\n\n**State After Race**:\n```\nfailure_count = 6  (incorrect, should be 7)\n```\n\n**With `asyncio.Lock`**:\n```\nTime  T0: Coroutine A acquires lock\nTime  T1: Coroutine B blocks on lock\nTime  T2: Coroutine A reads (5), increments (6), writes, releases lock\nTime  T3: Coroutine B acquires lock, reads (6), increments (7), writes, releases lock ✅\n```\n\n**Result**: All updates serialized, count is accurate.\n\n---\n\n#### Race 2.2: Increment-During-Read on `failure_count`\n\n**Scenario**: Status endpoint reads `failure_count` while it's being incremented.\n\n**Without Lock**:\n```\nTime  T0: failure_count = 10\nTime  T1: Coroutine A (status) reads failure_count → 10\nTime  T2: Coroutine B (failure handler) reads failure_count → 10\nTime  T3: Coroutine B increments: failure_count = 11\nTime  T4: Coroutine A returns status: {\"failure_count\": 10}  (stale)\n```\n\n**Impact**:\n- Status shows slightly stale count (acceptable for monitoring)\n- No correctness issue (count is monotonic, stale value is just outdated)\n\n**Verdict**: Acceptable for monitoring; no lock needed for reads.\n\n---\n\n### Category 3: Timestamp Assignment Races\n\n#### Race 3.1: Concurrent Write of `first_failure_timestamp`\n\n**Scenario**: Multiple failures attempt to set `first_failure_timestamp` simultaneously.\n\n**Without Lock**:\n```\nTime  T0: first_failure_timestamp = None\nTime  T1: Coroutine A checks first_failure_timestamp → None\nTime  T2: Coroutine B checks first_failure_timestamp → None\nTime  T3: Coroutine A sets first_failure_timestamp = datetime_A\nTime  T4: Coroutine B sets first_failure_timestamp = datetime_B ❌ OVERWRITE\nTime  T5: first_failure_timestamp = datetime_B  (non-deterministic)\n```\n\n**Impact**:\n- Lost first-failure timestamp accuracy\n- Difficult to correlate with logs\n- Misleading diagnostics (which was really first?)\n- Non-deterministic behavior\n\n**State After Race**:\n```\nfirst_failure_timestamp = datetime_B  (one of the two, non-deterministic)\n```\n\n**With `asyncio.Lock`**:\n```\nTime  T0: Coroutine A acquires lock\nTime  T1: Coroutine B blocks on lock\nTime  T2: Coroutine A checks → None, sets → datetime_A, releases lock\nTime  T3: Coroutine B acquires lock, checks → datetime_A (not None), skips set, releases lock ✅\n```\n\n**Result**: First timestamp is preserved exactly once.\n\n---\n\n#### Race 3.2: Write-During-Read on Timestamps\n\n**Scenario**: Status endpoint reads `first_failure_timestamp` while it's being written.\n\n**Without Lock**:\n```\nTime  T0: first_failure_timestamp = None\nTime  T1: Coroutine A (status) reads first_failure_timestamp → None\nTime  T2: Coroutine B (failure handler) sets first_failure_timestamp = now\nTime  T3: Coroutine A returns status: {\"first_failure_timestamp\": None}  (stale)\n```\n\n**Impact**:\n- Status shows stale timestamp (acceptable for monitoring)\n- No correctness issue (timestamp was None at read time)\n\n**Verdict**: Acceptable for monitoring.\n\n---\n\n### Category 4: Multi-Field Consistency Races\n\n#### Race 4.1: Inconsistent Snapshot of State\n\n**Scenario**: Multiple fields are updated as a group, but a read observes a partial update.\n\n**Without Lock**:\n```\nTime  T0: has_logged_first_failure = False, first_failure_timestamp = None, failure_count = 0\nTime  T1: Coroutine A (status) reads has_logged_first_failure → False\nTime  T2: Coroutine B (failure handler) sets has_logged_first_failure = True\nTime  T3: Coroutine B sets first_failure_timestamp = now_A\nTime  T4: Coroutine B sets failure_count = 1\nTime  T5: Coroutine A reads first_failure_timestamp → now_A\nTime  T6: Coroutine A reads failure_count → 1\nTime  T7: Coroutine A returns: {\"has_logged_first_failure\": False, \"first_failure_timestamp\": now_A, \"failure_count\": 1} ❌ INCONSISTENT\n```\n\n**Impact**:\n- Status shows inconsistent state (flag=False but timestamp=now_A)\n- Confusing diagnostics (was there a failure or not?)\n- Breaks assumptions about field relationships\n\n**State After Race**:\n```\nhas_logged_first_failure = True\nfirst_failure_timestamp = now_A\nfailure_count = 1\nStatus returned: False, now_A, 1  (inconsistent combination)\n```\n\n**With `asyncio.Lock`** (write path only):\n- Writes are serialized (all fields updated atomically under lock)\n- Reads may still see intermediate state (acceptable for monitoring)\n- If strict consistency is required, reads must also acquire lock\n\n**Verdict**: Acceptable for monitoring; critical decision logic requires lock.\n\n---\n\n#### Race 4.2: Notification Sent Flag Inconsistency\n\n**Scenario**: `notification_sent` flag and `notification_sent_at` timestamp are updated separately.\n\n**Without Lock**:\n```\nTime  T0: notification_sent = False, notification_sent_at = None\nTime  T1: Coroutine A (status) reads notification_sent → False\nTime  T2: Coroutine B (failure handler) sets notification_sent = True\nTime  T3: Coroutine B sets notification_sent_at = now_B\nTime  T4: Coroutine A reads notification_sent_at → now_B\nTime  T5: Coroutine A returns: {\"notification_sent\": False, \"notification_sent_at\": now_B} ❌ INCONSISTENT\n```\n\n**Impact**:\n- Status shows \"not sent\" but has a timestamp (contradiction)\n- Breaks assumptions about flag/timestamp relationship\n- Confusing diagnostics\n\n**With `asyncio.Lock`**:\n- All fields updated atomically under lock\n- Consistent snapshot guaranteed\n\n---\n\n### Category 5: Check-Then-Act on Reset Operations\n\n#### Race 5.1: Reset-During-Failure Handling\n\n**Scenario**: Admin resets state while failures are still being processed.\n\n**Without Lock**:\n```\nTime  T0: has_logged_first_failure = True, failure_count = 10\nTime  T1: Coroutine A (admin reset) checks has_logged_first_failure → True\nTime  T2: Coroutine B (failure handler) checks has_logged_first_failure → True\nTime  T3: Coroutine A sets has_logged_first_failure = False (reset)\nTime  T4: Coroutine A sets failure_count = 0 (reset)\nTime  T5: Coroutine B logs DEBUG (sees flag was True, now False) ❌ CONFUSING\nTime  T6: Coroutine B increments failure_count = 1 (based on stale check)\n```\n\n**Impact**:\n- Reset is partially undone by concurrent failure handler\n- State is inconsistent (flag=False, but failure_count was 10, now 1)\n- Confusing behavior\n\n**With `asyncio.Lock`**:\n- Reset and failure handling serialize\n- Either reset completes first, then failure handles new state\n- Or failure completes first, then reset clears it\n- Both produce consistent final state\n\n---\n\n#### Race 5.2: Concurrent Reset Calls\n\n**Scenario**: Two admins trigger reset simultaneously.\n\n**Without Lock**:\n```\nTime  T0: has_logged_first_failure = True, failure_count = 10\nTime  T1: Coroutine A (admin reset 1) reads has_logged_first_failure → True\nTime  T2: Coroutine B (admin reset 2) reads has_logged_first_failure → True\nTime  T3: Coroutine A sets has_logged_first_failure = False\nTime  T4: Coroutine A sets failure_count = 0\nTime  T5: Coroutine B sets has_logged_first_failure = False\nTime  T6: Coroutine B sets failure_count = 0  (redundant but harmless)\n```\n\n**Impact**:\n- Redundant resets (harmless if all fields are set to defaults)\n- No correctness issue, just inefficiency\n\n**Verdict**: Acceptable; redundant resets are idempotent.\n\n---\n\n### Category 6: Complex Multi-Step Operation Races\n\n#### Race 6.1: Failure Recording + Notification Sending\n\n**Scenario**: Recording first failure and sending notification is a multi-step operation.\n\n**Without Lock**:\n```\nTime  T0: has_logged_first_failure = False, notification_sent = False\nTime  T1: Coroutine A checks has_logged_first_failure → False\nTime  T2: Coroutine B checks has_logged_first_failure → False\nTime  T3: Coroutine A sets has_logged_first_failure = True\nTime  T4: Coroutine A sends notification (async HTTP call)\nTime  T5: Coroutine B sets has_logged_first_failure = True ❌ DUPLICATE\nTime  T6: Coroutine B sends notification ❌ DUPLICATE\n```\n\n**Impact**:\n- Duplicate notifications sent\n- State updated twice (redundant but harmless)\n- User receives multiple alerts for same event\n\n**With `asyncio.Lock`**:\n- Lock protects the entire \"check → update → notify\" sequence\n- Only one coroutine executes the full sequence\n- Others see `has_logged_first_failure = True` and skip notification\n\n**Verdict**: Critical race; lock is required.\n\n---\n\n#### Race 6.2: Failure Recording + Persistence (Database Write)\n\n**Scenario**: Recording first failure and persisting to database.\n\n**Without Lock**:\n```\nTime  T0: has_logged_first_failure = False\nTime  T1: Coroutine A checks has_logged_first_failure → False\nTime  T2: Coroutine B checks has_logged_first_failure → False\nTime  T3: Coroutine A sets has_logged_first_failure = True\nTime  T4: Coroutine A persists to DB (async I/O)\nTime  T5: Coroutine B sets has_logged_first_failure = True ❌ DUPLICATE\nTime  T6: Coroutine B persists to DB ❌ DUPLICATE\n```\n\n**Impact**:\n- Duplicate database records\n- Wasted storage\n- Inconsistent query results\n\n**With `asyncio.Lock`**:\n- Lock protects check-and-set (state mutation)\n- Persistence can happen outside lock (only if it's optional/slow)\n- Or lock protects entire sequence if persistence is critical\n\n**Design Decision**: If persistence is optional (monitoring only), it can happen outside lock. If persistence is critical (audit trail), it must be inside lock.\n\n---\n\n## Atomic vs Non-Atomic Operations\n\n### Atomic Operations (No Lock Needed)\n\nThese operations are inherently atomic in Python due to the GIL or immutability:\n\n1. **Reading immutable types**: `bool`, `int`, `float` reads are atomic at the bytecode level\n2. **Writing to a single variable**: Assignment to a simple variable is atomic\n3. **Reading/writing `asyncio.Lock` state**: Lock acquisition/release is atomic\n\n**Caveat**: While individual operations are atomic, **sequences** (check-then-act) are not.\n\n---\n\n### Non-Atomic Operations (Lock Required)\n\nThese operations require `asyncio.Lock` for thread-safety:\n\n1. **Check-then-act on `has_logged_first_failure`**:\n   ```python\n   if not self._has_logged_first_failure:  # ❌ Not atomic\n       self._has_logged_first_failure = True\n   ```\n\n2. **Read-modify-write on `failure_count`**:\n   ```python\n   self._failure_count += 1  # ❌ Not atomic (read, add, write)\n   ```\n\n3. **Multi-field updates**:\n   ```python\n   self._has_logged_first_failure = True\n   self._first_failure_timestamp = now  # ❌ Not atomic together\n   self._failure_count = 1\n   ```\n\n4. **Conditional updates based on multiple fields**:\n   ```python\n   if not self._has_logged_first_failure and self._failure_count == 0:  # ❌ Not atomic\n       # ...\n   ```\n\n---\n\n## Critical Sections Requiring Lock Protection\n\n### Critical Section 1: First-Failure Detection\n\n```python\nasync with self._first_failure_lock:\n    if not self._has_logged_first_failure:\n        # Entire block must be atomic\n        self._has_logged_first_failure = True\n        self._first_failure_timestamp = datetime.now(timezone.utc)\n        self._failure_count = 1\n        self._last_failure_timestamp = datetime.now(timezone.utc)\n        logger.warning(\"First failure...\")\n        await send_notification(...)  # If notification is critical\n```\n\n**Why**: Check-then-act on flag, multi-field update, notification sending.\n\n---\n\n### Critical Section 2: Failure Count Increment\n\n```python\nasync with self._first_failure_lock:\n    self._failure_count += 1  # Read-modify-write\n    self._last_failure_timestamp = datetime.now(timezone.utc)\n```\n\n**Why**: Increment is read-modify-write, timestamp update must be consistent.\n\n---\n\n### Critical Section 3: State Reset\n\n```python\nasync with self._first_failure_lock:\n    self._has_logged_first_failure = False\n    self._first_failure_timestamp = None\n    self._notification_sent = False\n    self._notification_sent_at = None\n    # Keep failure_count for diagnostics\n```\n\n**Why**: Multi-field reset must be atomic to avoid partial state.\n\n---\n\n### Non-Critical Sections (No Lock Required)\n\n1. **Status reads** (`get_bridge_status()`):\n   ```python\n   return {\n       \"has_logged_first_failure\": self._has_logged_first_failure,\n       \"failure_count\": self._failure_count,\n       # ... (reads are atomic, slight staleness is acceptable)\n   }\n   ```\n   **Why**: Reads are atomic; monitoring can tolerate slight staleness.\n\n2. **Logging operations**:\n   ```python\n   logger.debug(f\"Failure count: {self._failure_count}\")\n   ```\n   **Why**: Logging is side-effect-free for state.\n\n---\n\n## State Transition Conflicts\n\n### Conflict 1: First Failure vs Subsequent Failure\n\n**Transition**: `has_logged_first_failure: False → True`\n\n**Conflict**: Multiple concurrent failures race to be \"first\".\n\n**Resolution**: Lock ensures exactly one succeeds; others become \"subsequent\".\n\n---\n\n### Conflict 2: Subsequent Failure vs Reset\n\n**Transition**: `has_logged_first_failure: True → False` (reset)\n\n**Conflict**: Failure handler increments count while reset clears flag.\n\n**Resolution**: Lock serializes operations; reset happens either before or after increment.\n\n---\n\n### Conflict 3: Reset vs First Failure\n\n**Transition**: `has_logged_first_failure: False → True` (after reset)\n\n**Conflict**: Reset clears state while new failure sets it.\n\n**Resolution**: Lock serializes; whichever acquires lock first wins.\n\n---\n\n## Performance Implications of Locking\n\n### Lock Contention Analysis\n\n| Scenario | Lock Usage | Contention | Impact |\n|----------|-----------|-----------|--------|\n| Bridge healthy (no failures) | None | None | Zero ✅ |\n| First failure at startup | Single acquisition | None | Negligible (~1-2ms) |\n| Multiple concurrent failures | N acquisitions | Low-moderate | Acceptable (~2-5ms per request) |\n| High traffic with bridge down | Many acquisitions | Moderate | Bounded (queue wait = N × 2ms) |\n\n**Worst Case**: 100 concurrent failures = 200ms total queue wait = 2ms average per request.\n\n**Why this is acceptable**:\n- Bridge is already down (latency doesn't matter for failed sends)\n- Sends are already failing (no \"happy path\" to optimize)\n- Logging overhead dominates (2ms lock is <10% of total)\n\n---\n\n## Mitigation Strategy\n\n### Recommended: `asyncio.Lock` with Minimal Critical Section\n\n```python\nclass TelegramFallback:\n    def __init__(self):\n        self._first_failure_lock = asyncio.Lock()\n\n    async def _handle_send_failure(self, error_context: str = \"\"):\n        async with self._first_failure_lock:  # Serialize access\n            if not self._has_logged_first_failure:\n                # First failure - log at WARNING level\n                logger.warning(\"First failure...\")\n                self._has_logged_first_failure = True\n                self._first_failure_timestamp = datetime.now(timezone.utc)\n                self._failure_count = 1\n            else:\n                # Subsequent failure - log at DEBUG level\n                logger.debug(f\"Repeated failure #{self._failure_count + 1}\")\n                self._failure_count += 1\n            self._last_failure_timestamp = datetime.now(timezone.utc)\n```\n\n**Why this works**:\n- Lock protects the entire check-and-set sequence\n- Minimal overhead (lock held for microseconds)\n- No I/O inside lock (logging is fast, no DB/network calls)\n- Serializes the critical section only\n\n---\n\n## Testing Race Conditions\n\n### Test 1: Concurrent First Failures\n\n```python\n@pytest.mark.asyncio\nasync def test_concurrent_first_failures():\n    \"\"\"Verify only one WARNING is logged when multiple requests fail concurrently.\"\"\"\n    fallback = TelegramFallback()\n    \n    # Trigger 10 concurrent failures\n    await asyncio.gather(*[\n        fallback._handle_send_failure(f\"error{i}\")\n        for i in range(10)\n    ])\n    \n    # Verify state\n    assert fallback._has_logged_first_failure == True\n    assert fallback._failure_count == 10\n    \n    # Verify logs (count WARNING vs DEBUG)\n    # Should see: 1 WARNING, 9 DEBUG\n```\n\n### Test 2: Concurrent Increments\n\n```python\n@pytest.mark.asyncio\nasync def test_concurrent_increment_race():\n    \"\"\"Verify failure_count is accurate under concurrent increments.\"\"\"\n    fallback = TelegramFallback()\n    \n    # Simulate first failure\n    await fallback._handle_send_failure(\"first\")\n    \n    # Clear flag to allow increment-only race\n    fallback._has_logged_first_failure = True\n    \n    # Trigger 100 concurrent increments\n    await asyncio.gather(*[\n        fallback._handle_send_failure(f\"error{i}\")\n        for i in range(100)\n    ])\n    \n    # Verify no lost updates\n    assert fallback._failure_count == 101  # 1 + 100\n```\n\n### Test 3: Reset During Failures\n\n```python\n@pytest.mark.asyncio\nasync def test_reset_during_failures():\n    \"\"\"Verify reset and failures serialize correctly.\"\"\"\n    fallback = TelegramFallback()\n    \n    # Start with failures\n    await fallback._handle_send_failure(\"first\")\n    assert fallback._has_logged_first_failure == True\n    \n    # Concurrent reset and failure\n    reset_task = asyncio.create_task(fallback.reset_first_failure_state())\n    failure_task = asyncio.create_task(fallback._handle_send_failure(\"concurrent\"))\n    \n    await asyncio.gather(reset_task, failure_task)\n    \n    # State should be consistent (either reset happened or failure)\n    assert (fallback._has_logged_first_failure == True or \n            fallback._has_logged_first_failure == False)\n```\n\n---\n\n## Summary\n\n### All Identified Race Conditions\n\n| Category | Race | Impact | Mitigation |\n|----------|------|--------|------------|\n| Check-then-act | Concurrent first failures | Duplicate WARNING logs | `asyncio.Lock` |\n| Check-then-act | Read-during-write | Stale status data | Acceptable for monitoring |\n| Lost update | Concurrent increment | Inaccurate count | `asyncio.Lock` |\n| Lost update | Increment-during-read | Stale count | Acceptable for monitoring |\n| Timestamp | Concurrent timestamp write | Non-deterministic timestamp | `asyncio.Lock` |\n| Timestamp | Write-during-read | Stale timestamp | Acceptable for monitoring |\n| Multi-field | Inconsistent snapshot | Contradictory state | `asyncio.Lock` for writes |\n| Multi-field | Notification flag inconsistency | Contradictory state | `asyncio.Lock` |\n| Reset | Reset-during-failure | Partial undo | `asyncio.Lock` |\n| Reset | Concurrent reset | Redundant resets | Acceptable (idempotent) |\n| Multi-step | Failure + notification | Duplicate notifications | `asyncio.Lock` |\n| Multi-step | Failure + persistence | Duplicate DB records | `asyncio.Lock` (or outside if optional) |\n\n### Atomic Operations (No Lock)\n\n- Reading/writing single variables (bool, int)\n- Reading immutable types\n\n### Non-Atomic Operations (Lock Required)\n\n- Check-then-act sequences\n- Read-modify-write (increment)\n- Multi-field updates\n- Conditional updates based on multiple fields\n\n### Recommended Strategy\n\n1. **Use `asyncio.Lock`** for all state mutations (write operations)\n2. **No lock for reads** (monitoring can tolerate slight staleness)\n3. **Minimal critical section** (only state mutations, no I/O)\n4. **Test with `asyncio.gather()`** to verify race condition prevention\n\n### Performance Impact\n\n- **Happy path**: Zero overhead (no lock acquisition)\n- **Error path**: +2-5ms per request (negligible, only affects degraded state)\n- **Worst case**: ~200ms total wait for 100 concurrent failures (acceptable)\n\n---\n\n**Document**: adc-4ol5  \n**Status**: ✅ Complete  \n**Dependencies**: adc-50ld (thread-safety approach), adc-65l3 (state structure), adc-2duz (storage design)  \n**Date**: 2026-07-02\n","numLines":730,"startLine":1,"totalLines":730}}}

{"type":"stream_event","event":{"type":"content_block_delta","index":3,"delta":{"type":"input_json_delta","partial_json":"{\"file_path\":\"/home/coding/scratch/adc-4rh3-lock-bench.py\",\"content\":\"#!/usr/bin/env python3\\n\\\"\\\"\\\"\\nBenchmark: asyncio.Lock contention overhead for the adc-50ld/adc-41u0\\nfirst-failure locking strategy.\\n\\nMeasures (Python 3.12.12 on this box):\\n  1. Uncontended acquire+release of asyncio.Lock (the fast path — only one\\n     failure in flight).\\n  2. Contended throughput: N coroutines all serializing through one lock,\\n     each doing the *realistic* critical section (the `_record_failure_locked`\\n     body: a few attr writes + an int increment + a logging.debug call).\\n  3. Lock-free read path (get_bridge_status equivalent) for comparison.\\n  4. The SAME workload with NO lock at all, to isolate the lock's cost from\\n     the work itself.\\n\\nRun:  python3 ~/scratch/adc-4rh3-lock-bench.py\\n\\\"\\\"\\\"\\n\\nimport asyncio\\nimport logging\\nimport statistics\\nimport time\\nfrom datetime import datetime, timezone\\n\\n# Silence logging output during the timed loop; we want its *cost*, not its text.\\nlogging.basicConfig(level=logging.CRITICAL)\\nlogger = logging.getLogger(\\\"bench\\\")\\n\\n\\nclass Bench:\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"Mirrors the adc-41u0 design: instance lock + minimal no-I/O critical section.\\\"\\\"\\\"\\n\\n    def __init__(self):\\n        self._has_logged_first_failure = False\\n        self._failure_count = 0\\n        self._first_failure_ts = None\\n        self._last_failure_logged = None\\n        self._lock = asyncio.Lock()\\n\\n    # --- The real critical section (def, no await), per adc-41u0 §6.2 ---\\n    def _record_failure_locked(self, error_context: str) -> bool:\\n        self._failure_count += 1\\n        now = datetime.now(timezone.utc)\\n        if not self._has_logged_first_failure:\\n            self._has_logged_first_failure = True\\n            self._first_failure_ts = now\\n            self._last_failure_logged = now\\n            logger.warning(\\\"first %s\\\", error_context)  # WARNING is below CRITICAL threshold -> no-op\\n            return True\\n        self._last_failure_logged = now\\n        logger.debug(\\\"repeat #%d %s\\\", self._failure_count, error_context)\\n        return False\\n\\n    async def Uncontended acquire+release of asyncio.Lock (the fast path — only one\n     failure in flight).\n  2. Contended throughput: N coroutines all serializing through one lock,\n     each doing the *realistic* critical section (the `_record_failure_locked`\n     body: a few attr writes + an int increment + a logging.debug call).\n  3. Lock-free read path (get_bridge_status equivalent) for comparison.\n  4. The SAME workload with NO lock at all, to isolate the lock's cost from\n     the work itself.\n\nRun:  python3 ~/scratch/adc-4rh3-lock-bench.py\n\"\"\"\n\nimport asyncio\nimport logging\nimport statistics\nimport time\nfrom datetime import datetime, timezone\n\n# Silence logging output during the timed loop; we want its *cost*, not its text.\nlogging.basicConfig(level=logging.CRITICAL)\nlogger = logging.getLogger(\"bench\")\n\n\nclass Bench:\n    \"\"\"Mirrors the adc-41u0 design: instance lock + minimal no-I/O critical section.\"\"\"\n\n    def __init__(self):\n        self._has_logged_first_failure = False\n        self._failure_count = 0\n        self._first_failure_ts = None\n        self._last_failure_logged = None\n        self._lock = asyncio.Lock()\n\n    # --- The real critical section (def, no await), per adc-41u0 §6.2 ---\n    def _record_failure_locked(self, error_context: str) -> bool:\n self._first_failure_ts = now\n            self._last_failure_logged = now\n            logger.warning(\"first %s\", error_context)  # WARNING is below CRITICAL threshold -> no-op\n            return True\n        self._last_failure_logged = now\n        logger.debug(\"repeat #%d %s\", self._failure_count, error_context)\n        return False\n\n    async def handle_failure(self, error_context: str = \"e\") -> bool:\n        was_first = False\n        async with self._lock:\n            was_first = self._record_failure_locked(error_context)\n        return was_first\n\n    async def handle_failure_no_lock(self, error_context: str = \"e\") -> bool:\n        # Same work, no lock — isolates lock cost from work cost.\n        return self._record_failure_locked(error_context)\n\n bench_uncontended(n: int = 1_000_000) -> float:\n    \"\"\"Cost of one acquire+release when nobody else wants the lock.\"\"\"\n    lock = asyncio.Lock()\n    t0 = time.perf_counter_ns()\n    for _ in lock-free read path.\"\"\"\n    b = Bench()\n    t0 = time.perf_counter_ns()\n    for _ in range(n):\n        b.get_status()\n    t1 = time.perf_counter_ns()\n    return (t1 - t0) / n\n\n\nasync def bench_contended(workers: int, total_ops: int) -> float:\n    \"\"\"workers coroutines serialize through one lock; return ns/op (total wall-clock).\"\"\"\n    b = Bench()\n    per = total_ops // workers\n    done = asyncio.Event()\n    counter = {\"n\": 0}\n\n    async def runner():\n        local = 0\n        while counter[\"n\"] < total_ops:\n            await b.handle_failure(\"err\")\n asyncio.gather(*tasks)\n    t1 = time.perf_counter_ns()\n    return (t1 - t0) / counter[\"n\"], b._failure_count\n\n\nasync def bench_uncontended_with_work(n: int = 500_000) -> float:\n    \"\"\"Single coroutine, locked, doing the real work each time.\"\"\"\n    b = Bench()\n    t0 = time.perf_counter_ns()\n    for _ in range(n):\n        await b.handle_failure(\"err\")\n    t1 = from work cost.\"\"\"\n    b = Bench()\n    t0 = time.perf_counter_ns()\n    for _ in range(n):\n        await b.handle_failure_no_lock(\"err\")\n    t1 = time.perf_counter_ns()\n    return (t1 - t0) Lock-free read  (get_bridge_status equiv)     : {fmt_ns(rd)}\")\n\n    w_lock = await bench_uncontended_with_work()\n    w_nolock = await bench_uncontended_with_work_no_lock()\n    print(f\"3. Now I have real, measured numbers. Let me summarize the findings:\n\n**Measured results (Python 3.12.12, this Hetzner box):**\n1. Uncontended lock acquire+release: **~468 ns (0.47 µs)**\n2. Lock-free read: **~94 ns (0.09 µs)**\n3. Realistic critical section with lock (1 coroutine): **~925 ns (0.92 µs)**\n4. Same work without lock: **~604 ns**\n5. **Isolated lock overhead: ~321 ns (0.32 µs)** — this is the actual tax the lock adds per failure\n6. Contended throughput stays flat at **~1.0 µs/op from 2 to 128 concurrent workers** (the critical section has no `await`, so holders don't yield mid-section; throughput is bounded by the work cost, not by contention queuing).\n\n**The critical correction:** The existing docs (adc-50ld §\"Performance Implications\" and adc-4ol5 §\"Performance Implications of Locking\") claim:\n- \"First failure ... Single acquisition for ~1-2ms\"\n- \"Subsequent failure ... +2-5ms per request\"\n- \"100 concurrent failures = 200ms total queue wait = 2ms average per request\"\n- Worst case \"N × 2ms\"\n\nThese numbers are **wrong by ~4,000x**. They appear to have conflated *logging I/O latency* (a `logger.warning` to a slow handler could be ms) with *lock acquisition cost* (which is sub-microsecond). The actual isolated lock overhead is ~320 ns, and even a fully-contended 128-way workload completes 200k serialized ops in ~200ms total — meaning 100 concurrent failures would serialize in **~100 µs total**, not 200ms.\n\nWait, let me reconsider the \"100 concurrent failures\" worst-case from the existing docs. They said \"200ms total queue wait\". Let me compute the real number: 100 failures, each critical section ~925 ns with lock (604 ns work + 321 ns lock). Serialized: 100 × 925 ns = **92.5 µs total wall-clock** (if they all arrive at once and serialize). That's ~2,000x faster than the doc's \"200ms\" claim.\n\nActually, the more precise statement: in an asyncio single-loop model, \"100 concurrent failures\" don't even truly run in parallel — they're coroutines. If they all hit `send_message` and fail concurrently (e.g., 100 HTTP requests in flight when the bridge goes down), they each reach `_handle_send_failure` and serialize through the lock. Each holds the lock for ~600 ns of work (no await inside), so the total serialization is ~60 µs. The doc's 200ms is off by ~3,000x.\n\nSo the central finding of my doc: **The performance impact of the chosen locking strategy is, by measurement, negligible — sub-microsecond per failure, flat under contention up to 128-way concurrency. The existing design docs' \"2-5ms / 200ms worst case\" estimates are not just pessimistic, they're wrong by 3-4 orders of magnitude, and they conflate lock cost with logging-I/O cost.** The correctness gains (no duplicate WARNINGs, accurate count) come essentially free.\n\nAlso important: even if I'm wrong about the absolute numbers, the *dormant path* argument dominates. The lock-protected code path has **zero live callers today** (send_message → send_result has no callers; send_exception/send_workload_summary are no-op stubs). So the realistic contention is exactly zero until that path is wired into the dispatch pipeline. This is the strongest possible \"no performance concern\" argument.\n\nNow let me also address the other acceptance criteria:\n- **Bottlenecks / hot paths**: The only hot path is `send_message`'s 3 failure branches → `_handle_send_failure`. And it's dormant. The status read path is lock-free. So there are no performance-critical code paths acquiring this lock in production today.\n- **Performance vs correctness trade-off**: The lock buys correctness (no duplicate WARNINGs, accurate count, consistent multi-field snapshot) at a cost of ~321 ns per failure. Since failures are (a) on the error path and (b) on a dormant channel, the trade-off is overwhelmingly in favor of correctness. There's no realistic scenario where you'd trade away the lock for speed here.\n- **Acceptable vs unacceptable thresholds**: Define a budget. E.g., acceptable: lock adds <1% of a dispatch round-trip (dispatch involves multiple LLM calls = seconds). The ~321ns lock is ~0.00003% of even a 1-second dispatch. Unacceptable would be: holding the lock across I/O (notification HTTP send = seconds), which would serialize every concurrent failing request behind one HTTP timeout — that's the one real risk, and it's structurally prevented by the `def`-helper rule.\n- **Mitigation strategies**: (1) the no-I/O-in-critical-section rule (already in adc-41u0, enforced by `def` helper); (2) lock-free reads (already chosen); (3) if the path ever becomes hot AND high-frequency, the SQLite-atomic upgrade path (adc-4783 §5b) removes the lock entirely while staying correct.\n\nLet me also note one subtle performance point: the `asyncio.Lock` is created in `__init__`, which runs on the serving loop (lifespan), so there's no loop-binding cost or hazard.\n\nNow let me write the doc. I'll put it at `notes/adc-4rh3.md`. It should:\n1. State the measured metrics prominently (correcting the prior estimates)\n2. Identify the hot paths (and the dormancy finding)\n3. Performance vs correctness trade-off\n4. Acceptable/unacceptable thresholds\n5. Mitigation strategies\n6. Cross-reference adc-41u0, adc-4783, adc-50ld, adc-4ol5\n7. Include the benchmark script as an appendix for reproducibility\n8. Note the benchmark file location in scratch/\n\nLet me write it now.
